pages.title: Meet Your 24/7 AI Employee
pages.folder: Day 01 — Get Hermes-Ready
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Meet Your 24/7 AI Employee What you'll have by the end: A clear map of everything you're going to build in this course — a personal chief of staff, a mission control dashboard, a full team of AI agents — and the exact business model behind it: done-for-you digital employees that people are selling for $500–$5,000/month to set up, or $200–$1,000/month just to maintain.

 Why this matters: The gap between people who use these agents and people who don't is getting a lot wider, and most companies have no idea this even exists yet. Understanding what Hermes Agent actually is (and isn't) is the foundation for every build in this course.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps This is an orientation chapter — no installs yet. Your job today is to lock in the mental models Samin lays out, because everything else in the course is built on them.

 - Understand what Hermes Agent is. Take the smartest AI model available (ChatGPT, Claude, Grok — whichever), give it eyes, hands, and sit it at a desk with a mouse connected to a computer. Now you can message it like a co-worker, and it can do anything a regular person could do on that computer — on its own, without you asking constantly.
 - Know why it's different from OpenClaw, Claude Code, and Codex. OpenClaw is "an AI agent that can use your computer." Hermes is "an AI employee that grows with you over time." The key is its self-improvement loop : when Hermes learns how you do something (how you edit videos, how you write in your tone), it automatically saves that process as a reusable skill — without you asking. Next time you give it the same kind of task, it's already learned from the last session.
 - Understand the memory curator. Roughly every 10 turns of conversation, Hermes runs a background process that reviews what you're doing, improves its own memory, cleans up old skills, and archives what's no longer useful. That's why it doesn't get bloated and break over time the way OpenClaw used to — it strategically archives instead of blindly remembering everything.
 - Lock in the car analogy (model vs. harness). The model (GPT 5.5, Claude Opus, Grok, Google Gemini) is the engine — powerful, but it just sits there on its own. Hermes is the rest of the car — the agentic harness that wraps the model so it can actually do work: control your computer, run 24/7, message you through different channels, and keep its own memory system. Claude Code and Codex are great harnesses too, but they were built to code. Hermes was built to be an employee that knows you, your business, your customers, and your workflows.
 - Note the two structural advantages. Hermes is model agnostic (you're never locked into one provider, so you can always use the best model for each task as they leapfrog each other) and it's local (all its data lives on your computer, right next to you).
 - Take the risks seriously. You are still the one in charge. These agents are powerful but they are not autonomous decision makers — one researcher gave her agent access to her email inbox and it deleted years of emails. That really happened. It's on you to verify outputs before you trust them. Samin will show you how to put safeguards in place before your agent ever sends an email, moves money, or publishes anything.
 - Write down the business model. People are already selling the exact systems you'll build in this course to businesses as done-for-you digital employees : $500–$5,000/month for setup, or $200–$1,000/month just to maintain. The business owner doesn't need to understand how it works — they just get an agent running 24/7 at a fraction of what a human costs.
 - Commit to the one rule. If you're brand new, watch the course in order. If you already run Hermes Agent, use the video timestamps to jump around. Either way, follow Samin's single recommendation: build alongside him .
 Copy-paste prompts No prompts in this chapter — the hands-on prompting starts when you set up Hermes from scratch in the next section.

 ✅ Your Day 1 post Post in the community: the one system from the course roadmap you're most excited to build (chief of staff, social media agent, trading analyst, etc.) and the type of business you'd sell it to as a done-for-you digital employee.

🎬 Watch the video above, then follow the steps below.

Meet Your 24/7 AI Employee

What you'll have by the end: A clear map of everything you're going to build in this course — a personal chief of staff, a mission control dashboard, a full team of AI agents — and the exact business model behind it: done-for-you digital employees that people are selling for $500–$5,000/month to set up, or $200–$1,000/month just to maintain.

Why this matters: The gap between people who use these agents and people who don't is getting a lot wider, and most companies have no idea this even exists yet. Understanding what Hermes Agent actually is (and isn't) is the foundation for every build in this course.

Watch the video, then follow these steps

This is an orientation chapter — no installs yet. Your job today is to lock in the mental models Samin lays out, because everything else in the course is built on them.

  1. Understand what Hermes Agent is. Take the smartest AI model available (ChatGPT, Claude, Grok — whichever), give it eyes, hands, and sit it at a desk with a mouse connected to a computer. Now you can message it like a co-worker, and it can do anything a regular person could do on that computer — on its own, without you asking constantly.
  1. Know why it's different from OpenClaw, Claude Code, and Codex. OpenClaw is "an AI agent that can use your computer." Hermes is "an AI employee that grows with you over time." The key is its self-improvement loop: when Hermes learns how you do something (how you edit videos, how you write in your tone), it automatically saves that process as a reusable skill — without you asking. Next time you give it the same kind of task, it's already learned from the last session.
  1. Understand the memory curator. Roughly every 10 turns of conversation, Hermes runs a background process that reviews what you're doing, improves its own memory, cleans up old skills, and archives what's no longer useful. That's why it doesn't get bloated and break over time the way OpenClaw used to — it strategically archives instead of blindly remembering everything.
  1. Lock in the car analogy (model vs. harness). The model (GPT 5.5, Claude Opus, Grok, Google Gemini) is the engine — powerful, but it just sits there on its own. Hermes is the rest of the car — the agentic harness that wraps the model so it can actually do work: control your computer, run 24/7, message you through different channels, and keep its own memory system. Claude Code and Codex are great harnesses too, but they were built to code. Hermes was built to be an employee that knows you, your business, your customers, and your workflows.
  1. Note the two structural advantages. Hermes is model agnostic (you're never locked into one provider, so you can always use the best model for each task as they leapfrog each other) and it's local (all its data lives on your computer, right next to you).
  1. Take the risks seriously. You are still the one in charge. These agents are powerful but they are not autonomous decision makers — one researcher gave her agent access to her email inbox and it deleted years of emails. That really happened. It's on you to verify outputs before you trust them. Samin will show you how to put safeguards in place before your agent ever sends an email, moves money, or publishes anything.
  1. Write down the business model. People are already selling the exact systems you'll build in this course to businesses as done-for-you digital employees: $500–$5,000/month for setup, or $200–$1,000/month just to maintain. The business owner doesn't need to understand how it works — they just get an agent running 24/7 at a fraction of what a human costs.
  1. Commit to the one rule. If you're brand new, watch the course in order. If you already run Hermes Agent, use the video timestamps to jump around. Either way, follow Samin's single recommendation: build alongside him.

Copy-paste prompts

No prompts in this chapter — the hands-on prompting starts when you set up Hermes from scratch in the next section.

✅ Your Day 1 post

Post in the community: the one system from the course roadmap you're most excited to build (chief of staff, social media agent, trading analyst, etc.) and the type of business you'd sell it to as a done-for-you digital employee.

But Hermes is the agentic harness that comes bundled with the ability to let's say control everything on your computer. It runs on the machine 24/7. It has the gateways so you can start messaging it on all the different channels and it can get work done like an employee and it's it has its own memory system. So there are other really good harnesses out there like claude code or codeex but there's a reason why I use Hermes as my chief of staff and not only something like cloud code. You see these tools like cloud code were designed for different jobs fundamentally cloud code like the name suggests was built to code. Hermes agent on the other hand was designed to be an AI employee built to know you your business your customers your preferences and all your workflows over time because of how its memory system was built. Also, another big advantage is that Hermes is model agnostic and it's local. That means all its data lives on your computer right next to you and you're not

locked into one provider. And since these models are always leapfrogging each other, Hermes agent lets you be nimble. You always get to use the best model for each specific task. And now that's all good. Let's cover the bad and the ugly. Because you see, you're still the one in charge. Because there's a version of this where it sounds like you hand Hermes the keys and you disappear. But that's not really how any of this works. Because these agents are powerful, but they're not autonomous decision makers. You can't let it loose without structure. And it's on you to give it the right access and to protect yourself from giving it too much too fast. For example, a researcher gave her agent access to her email inbox and the agent just deleted it. She lost years of emails and that actually happened. And that's why it's on you to verify the outputs before you trust them. And I'll show you how to put all these safeguards in place before you let your agent send an email, move any money, or publish anything. But the reality right now is that the gap between people who use these agents and people who don't is getting a lot wider. Every company is going to need something like this, and most have no idea that it barely even exists or how to even start with it. But the people who understand this right now, while it's still early, are the ones

who end up building businesses and careers everyone wish they started before. The same systems we're going to be building in this course, people are already selling to other businesses as done for you digital employees, and they charge from 500 to 5,000 a month to set it up or $200 to $1,000 a month just to maintain it. The business owner doesn't need to understand how this works. They just get an agent that runs 24/7 at a fraction of what a human would cost. And we'll go later into this with the exact pricing, offer structures, and full endto-end examples later in the course.

rag.search_text:
Meet Your 24/7 AI Employee

Day 01 — Get Hermes-Ready

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Meet Your 24/7 AI Employee What you'll have by the end: A clear map of everything you're going to build in this course — a personal chief of staff, a mission control dashboard, a full team of AI agents — and the exact business model behind it: done-for-you digital employees that people are selling for $500–$5,000/month to set up, or $200–$1,000/month just to maintain.

 Why this matters: The gap between people who use these agents and people who don't is getting a lot wider, and most companies have no idea this even exists yet. Understanding what Hermes Agent actually is (and isn't) is the foundation for every build in this course.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps This is an orientation chapter — no installs yet. Your job today is to lock in the mental models Samin lays out, because everything else in the course is built on them.

 - Understand what Hermes Agent is. Take the smartest AI model available (ChatGPT, Claude, Grok — whichever), give it eyes, hands, and sit it at a desk with a mouse connected to a computer. Now you can message it like a co-worker, and it can do anything a regular person could do on that computer — on its own, without you asking constantly.
 - Know why it's different from OpenClaw, Claude Code, and Codex. OpenClaw is "an AI agent that can use your computer." Hermes is "an AI employee that grows with you over time." The key is its self-improvement loop : when Hermes learns how you do something (how you edit videos, how you write in your tone), it automatically saves that process as a reusable skill — without you asking. Next time you give it the same kind of task, it's already learned from the last session.
 - Understand the memory curator. Roughly every 10 turns of conversation, Hermes runs a background process that reviews what you're doing, improves its own memory, cleans up old skills, and archives what's no longer useful. That's why it doesn't get bloated and break over time the way OpenClaw used to — it strategically archives instead of blindly remembering everything.
 - Lock in the car analogy (model vs. harness). The model (GPT 5.5, Claude Opus, Grok, Google Gemini) is the engine — powerful, but it just sits there on its own. Hermes is the rest of the car — the agentic harness that wraps the model so it can actually do work: control your computer, run 24/7, message you through different channels, and keep its own memory system. Claude Code and Codex are great harnesses too, but they were built to code. Hermes was built to be an employee that knows you, your business, your customers, and your workflows.
 - Note the two structural advantages. Hermes is model agnostic (you're never locked into one provider, so you can always use the best model for each task as they leapfrog each other) and it's local (all its data lives on your computer, right next to you).
 - Take the risks seriously. You are still the one in charge. These agents are powerful but they are not autonomous decision makers — one researcher gave her agent access to her email inbox and it deleted years of emails. That really happened. It's on you to verify outputs before you trust them. Samin will show you how to put safeguards in place before your agent ever sends an email, moves money, or publishes anything.
 - Write down the business model. People are already selling the exact systems you'll build in this course to businesses as done-for-you digital employees : $500–$5,000/month for setup, or $200–$1,000/month just to maintain. The business owner doesn't need to understand how it works — they just get an agent running 24/7 at a fraction of what a human costs.
 - Commit to the one rule. If you're brand new, watch the course in order. If you already run Hermes Agent, use the video timestamps to jump around. Either way, follow Samin's single recommendation: build alongside him .
 Copy-paste prompts No prompts in this chapter — the hands-on prompting starts when you set up Hermes from scratch in the next section.

 ✅ Your Day 1 post Post in the community: the one system from the course roadmap you're most excited to build (chief of staff, social media agent, trading analyst, etc.) and the type of business you'd sell it to as a done-for-you digital employee.

pages.title: Set Up Your Computer & Repair Buddy
pages.folder: Day 01 — Get Hermes-Ready
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Set Up Your Computer & Repair Buddy What you'll have by the end 

 The Codex desktop app installed on your computer and logged in with your ChatGPT account. That's your "repair buddy" — an AI doctor that can fix anything that breaks, even from your phone — set up before Hermes ever touches your machine.

 Why this matters 

 Every new tool breaks eventually, and beginners quit when it does. With Codex installed first, you never get stuck: whatever goes wrong, you just ask Codex to fix it and move on.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 

 There are three decisions in this chapter, then one install. Samin walks through each one on screen.

 - Choose where Hermes will live. You have two paths: a VPS (a server you rent in the cloud) or a personal computer — a Mac Mini, an old computer, or a PC. Mac or Windows both work. Samin recommends and demonstrates the personal computer path on a Mac, because you pay once for the hardware, you get an easy interface, and you can see exactly what's happening on your screen. (The cons of a home computer — it has to stay powered on, and access is limited when you're away — get solved in step 5.)
 - Choose the brain. Hermes needs intelligence to run on. Samin recommends the ChatGPT plans from OpenAI — the most bang for buck in intelligence, accessibility, and ability.
 - Pick flat-cost billing, not API billing. There are two ways to pay for that intelligence. Option one: a ChatGPT subscription, which gives you effectively unlimited usage within a time window — your costs stay flat no matter how much Hermes runs. Option two: API token billing (pay per usage — this is what's happening when something asks you for an API key). Skip the API for now. If something runs that you didn't intend, API billing can rack up hundreds or even thousands of dollars in credits. Go to the ChatGPT subscription page and pick a plan — you can even start free and upgrade as you need.
 - Install Codex — your buddy system. Hermes and Codex share the same brain (GPT 5.5) through your ChatGPT subscription, so you're already paying for Codex. One brain, two roles. To install it:
 - Go to Codex by ChatGPT (all links are below the video).
- Download the desktop app for Mac or Windows — whichever you're using. It also works on a VPS.
- Open it and log in with your ChatGPT account .
- When you see the screen Samin shows in the video, you're good to go.
 - Know why Codex fixes the home-computer cons. A recent Codex update lets it securely use apps on your Mac from your phone through the ChatGPT app — even when your Mac is locked and the screen is off. So you can be anywhere in the world and still control your computer. That means one hardware payment gets you everything: no monthly server bill, and no "I'm away from my desk so I'm stuck" problem.
 The workflow from here on is simple: a problem happens, you ask Codex to fix it, Codex fixes it, you apply the fix and move on. Codex is the doctor for your Hermes agent — think of it as insurance.

 Copy-paste prompts 

 No reusable prompts in this chapter — the win here is decisions plus one install. Prompts start flowing in the next chapters.

 Troubleshooting 

 If you get stuck anywhere in this course — on a VPS, on a Mac, on Windows, wherever — the answer is always the same: ask Codex to fix it. Describe the problem, let Codex propose the fix, apply it, and move on. That's the whole buddy system.

 ✅ Your Day 1 post 

 Post a screenshot of the Codex desktop app open and logged in with your ChatGPT account, plus one line saying which path you chose (Mac Mini / old computer / VPS).

 Every new tool breaks eventually, and beginners quit when it does. With Codex installed first, you never get stuck: whatever goes wrong, you just ask Codex to fix it and move on.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 

 There are three decisions in this chapter, then one install. Samin walks through each one on screen.

 - Choose where Hermes will live. You have two paths: a VPS (a server you rent in the cloud) or a personal computer — a Mac Mini, an old computer, or a PC. Mac or Windows both work. Samin recommends and demonstrates the personal computer path on a Mac, because you pay once for the hardware, you get an easy interface, and you can see exactly what's happening on your screen. (The cons of a home computer — it has to stay powered on, and access is limited when you're away — get solved in step 5.)
 - Choose the brain. Hermes needs intelligence to run on. Samin recommends the ChatGPT plans from OpenAI — the most bang for buck in intelligence, accessibility, and ability.
 - Pick flat-cost billing, not API billing. There are two ways to pay for that intelligence. Option one: a ChatGPT subscription, which gives you effectively unlimited usage within a time window — your costs stay flat no matter how much Hermes runs. Option two: API token billing (pay per usage — this is what's happening when something asks you for an API key). Skip the API for now. If something runs that you didn't intend, API billing can rack up hundreds or even thousands of dollars in credits. Go to the ChatGPT subscription page and pick a plan — you can even start free and upgrade as you need.
 - Install Codex — your buddy system. Hermes and Codex share the same brain (GPT 5.5) through your ChatGPT subscription, so you're already paying for Codex. One brain, two roles. To install it:
 - Go to Codex by ChatGPT (all links are below the video).
- Download the desktop app for Mac or Windows — whichever you're using. It also works on a VPS.
- Open it and log in with your ChatGPT account .
- When you see the screen Samin shows in the video, you're good to go.
 - Know why Codex fixes the home-computer cons. A recent Codex update lets it securely use apps on your Mac from your phone through the ChatGPT app — even when your Mac is locked and the screen is off. So you can be anywhere in the world and still control your computer. That means one hardware payment gets you everything: no monthly server bill, and no "I'm away from my desk so I'm stuck" problem.
 The workflow from here on is simple: a problem happens, you ask Codex to fix it, Codex fixes it, you apply the fix and move on. Codex is the doctor for your Hermes agent — think of it as insurance.

 Copy-paste prompts 

 No reusable prompts in this chapter — the win here is decisions plus one install. Prompts start flowing in the next chapters.

 Troubleshooting 

 If you get stuck anywhere in this course — on a VPS, on a Mac, on Windows, wherever — the answer is always the same: ask Codex to fix it. Describe the problem, let Codex propose the fix, apply it, and move on. That's the whole buddy system.

 ✅ Your Day 1 post 

 Post a screenshot of the Codex desktop app open and logged in with your ChatGPT account, plus one line saying which path you chose (Mac Mini / old computer / VPS).

pages.folder: Day 02 — Install Hermes & Connect Its Brain
course_path: Day 02 — Install Hermes & Connect Its Brain
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Install Hermes & Connect Its Brain What you'll have by the end 

 Hermes Agent installed on your Mac with GPT 5.5 connected as its primary brain, News Portal set up as the backup, a first successful chat, and (optionally) full computer-use permissions so Hermes can operate your machine for you.

 Why this matters 

 Everything else in this course runs on top of this install. Once the brain is connected and permissions are granted, Hermes stops being a chatbot and starts being an employee that can actually use your computer.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 

 - Download Hermes Agent. Google "Hermes Agent" (or use the link below the video) and open the Hermes Agent page. If you're on a desktop — which is much easier — hit the download button and follow the on-screen setup directions.
 - Install and launch it. Open the downloaded app and hit Install Hermes . Let it do its thing. When it's done, you can launch it from anywhere — just open Hermes Desktop and hit Launch Hermes . The app starts up like any normal Mac application.
 - Take the quick tour. Inside the desktop app you'll see the main pieces you'll use all course: New Session (start a fresh chat), Skills (all your skills in one place), and Channels (everything you'll connect Hermes to later — this becomes really important as we go).
 - Open Settings and set up your model provider. The first thing to configure is the provider for your models. We want OpenAI Codex as the primary.
 - Connect OpenAI Codex. In Settings, hit the OpenAI Codex setup button. A window comes up to authenticate with your ChatGPT account — one click. ChatGPT gives you a code; go back to Hermes, paste it in, and hit Continue . That's it — GPT 5.5 is now your primary model.
 - Add News Portal as your backup brain. News Portal is the company behind Hermes Agent. Samin uses it as his fallback for three reasons: you instantly get access to all the top-tier models, you don't need 200 different subscriptions (image models, audio models — it's all in there), and it's a backup brain if ChatGPT is ever down or you run out of credits. To set it up: in Settings, hit News Portal , then Set up News Portal — it connects in one click. Just make sure you have a News Portal account first.
 - Dial in the model settings. You can now switch between OpenAI Codex and all the News Portal models. Enable fast mode and set it to high — Samin has found this is the best mix and the most cost-effective setup. GPT 5.5 stays as the first model it uses. For the fallback slot, put News Portal and pick something like Claude Opus — or GLM 5.2 if you want to go local. So you end up with two references and a fallback chain.
 - Test it with a first chat. Hit New Session . At the bottom of the chat box, select your model — GPT 5.5 fast high (you'll see all your models listed here, and where each one is running from). Then send a simple message:
 Copy-paste prompt
hello If it responds, your brain is connected. That's your quick win.

 - (Optional but recommended) Enable computer use. This step tells Hermes: "this computer belongs to you." It makes everything Hermes does for you significantly simpler — you can just talk to it and it gets things done without you touching the machine. In your session, send:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, can you help me set up Hermes computer use? Hermes will start installing a package that lets it control and access your computer whenever it needs to do something for you.

 - Grant the macOS permissions. Hermes will ask for permissions — give it everything it needs, including the driver access permission it requests (follow the on-screen steps in the video). Hit Enable , then Quit and Restart when prompted. After the restart, Hermes Agent should be able to use your computer.
 Copy-paste prompts 

 Hey, can you help me set up Hermes computer use? Troubleshooting 

 Samin hits a real snag in the video: Hermes says computer-use setup isn't fully done, even though the permissions were already granted in macOS settings. The fix is simple — screenshot your permission settings and send the screenshot to Hermes in the chat. Tell it the permissions are turned on, let it look at the screenshot, and ask it to verify again. That basic loop — turn the setting on, screenshot it, hand it to the agent — solves most permission hiccups.

 ✅ Your Day 2 post 

 Post a screenshot of your first successful Hermes chat (your "hello" and its reply, with GPT 5.5 selected as the model) in the community — bonus points if you mention your backup model is configured too.

🎬 Watch the video above, then follow the steps below.

Install Hermes & Connect Its Brain

What you'll have by the end

Hermes Agent installed on your Mac with GPT 5.5 connected as its primary brain, News Portal set up as the backup, a first successful chat, and (optionally) full computer-use permissions so Hermes can operate your machine for you.

Why this matters

Everything else in this course runs on top of this install. Once the brain is connected and permissions are granted, Hermes stops being a chatbot and starts being an employee that can actually use your computer.

Watch the video, then follow these steps

  1. Download Hermes Agent. Google "Hermes Agent" (or use the link below the video) and open the Hermes Agent page. If you're on a desktop — which is much easier — hit the download button and follow the on-screen setup directions.
  1. Install and launch it. Open the downloaded app and hit Install Hermes. Let it do its thing. When it's done, you can launch it from anywhere — just open Hermes Desktop and hit Launch Hermes. The app starts up like any normal Mac application.
  1. Take the quick tour. Inside the desktop app you'll see the main pieces you'll use all course: New Session (start a fresh chat), Skills (all your skills in one place), and Channels (everything you'll connect Hermes to later — this becomes really important as we go).
  1. Open Settings and set up your model provider. The first thing to configure is the provider for your models. We want OpenAI Codex as the primary.
  1. Connect OpenAI Codex. In Settings, hit the OpenAI Codex setup button. A window comes up to authenticate with your ChatGPT account — one click. ChatGPT gives you a code; go back to Hermes, paste it in, and hit Continue. That's it — GPT 5.5 is now your primary model.
  1. Add News Portal as your backup brain. News Portal is the company behind Hermes Agent. Samin uses it as his fallback for three reasons: you instantly get access to all the top-tier models, you don't need 200 different subscriptions (image models, audio models — it's all in there), and it's a backup brain if ChatGPT is ever down or you run out of credits. To set it up: in Settings, hit News Portal, then Set up News Portal — it connects in one click. Just make sure you have a News Portal account first.
  1. Dial in the model settings. You can now switch between OpenAI Codex and all the News Portal models. Enable fast mode and set it to high — Samin has found this is the best mix and the most cost-effective setup. GPT 5.5 stays as the first model it uses. For the fallback slot, put News Portal and pick something like Claude Opus — or GLM 5.2 if you want to go local. So you end up with two references and a fallback chain.
  1. Test it with a first chat. Hit New Session. At the bottom of the chat box, select your model — GPT 5.5 fast high (you'll see all your models listed here, and where each one is running from). Then send a simple message:
Copy-paste prompt
hello

If it responds, your brain is connected. That's your quick win.

  1. (Optional but recommended) Enable computer use. This step tells Hermes: "this computer belongs to you." It makes everything Hermes does for you significantly simpler — you can just talk to it and it gets things done without you touching the machine. In your session, send:
Copy-paste prompt
Hey, can you help me set up Hermes computer use?

Hermes will start installing a package that lets it control and access your computer whenever it needs to do something for you.

  1. Grant the macOS permissions. Hermes will ask for permissions — give it everything it needs, including the driver access permission it requests (follow the on-screen steps in the video). Hit Enable, then Quit and Restart when prompted. After the restart, Hermes Agent should be able to use your computer.

Copy-paste prompts

Hey, can you help me set up Hermes computer use?

Troubleshooting

Samin hits a real snag in the video: Hermes says computer-use setup isn't fully done, even though the permissions were already granted in macOS settings. The fix is simple — screenshot your permission settings and send the screenshot to Hermes in the chat. Tell it the permissions are turned on, let it look at the screenshot, and ask it to verify again. That basic loop — turn the setting on, screenshot it, hand it to the agent — solves most permission hiccups.

✅ Your Day 2 post

Post a screenshot of your first successful Hermes chat (your "hello" and its reply, with GPT 5.5 selected as the model) in the community — bonus points if you mention your backup model is configured too.

rag.search_text:
Install Hermes & Connect Its Brain

Day 02 — Install Hermes & Connect Its Brain

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Install Hermes & Connect Its Brain What you'll have by the end 

 Hermes Agent installed on your Mac with GPT 5.5 connected as its primary brain, News Portal set up as the backup, a first successful chat, and (optionally) full computer-use permissions so Hermes can operate your machine for you.

 Why this matters 

 Everything else in this course runs on top of this install. Once the brain is connected and permissions are granted, Hermes stops being a chatbot and starts being an employee that can actually use your computer.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 

 - Download Hermes Agent. Google "Hermes Agent" (or use the link below the video) and open the Hermes Agent page. If you're on a desktop — which is much easier — hit the download button and follow the on-screen setup directions.
 - Install and launch it. Open the downloaded app and hit Install Hermes . Let it do its thing. When it's done, you can launch it from anywhere — just open Hermes Desktop and hit Launch Hermes . The app starts up like any normal Mac application.
 - Take the quick tour. Inside the desktop app you'll see the main pieces you'll use all course: New Session (start a fresh chat), Skills (all your skills in one place), and Channels (everything you'll connect Hermes to later — this becomes really important as we go).
 - Open Settings and set up your model provider. The first thing to configure is the provider for your models. We want OpenAI Codex as the primary.
 - Connect OpenAI Codex. In Settings, hit the OpenAI Codex setup button. A window comes up to authenticate with your ChatGPT account — one click. ChatGPT gives you a code; go back to Hermes, paste it in, and hit Continue . That's it — GPT 5.5 is now your primary model.
 - Add News Portal as your backup brain. News Portal is the company behind Hermes Agent. Samin uses it as his fallback for three reasons: you instantly get access to all the top-tier models, you don't need 200 different subscriptions (image models, audio models — it's all in there), and it's a backup brain if ChatGPT is ever down or you run out of credits. To set it up: in Settings, hit News Portal , then Set up News Portal — it connects in one click. Just make sure you have a News Portal account first.
 - Dial in the model settings. You can now switch between OpenAI Codex and all the News Portal models. Enable fast mode and set it to high — Samin has found this is the best mix and the most cost-effective setup. GPT 5.5 stays as the first model it uses. For the fallback slot, put News Portal and pick something like Claude Opus — or GLM 5.2 if you want to go local. So you end up with two references and a fallback chain.
 - Test it with a first chat. Hit New Session . At the bottom of the chat box, select your model — GPT 5.5 fast high (you'll see all your models listed here, and where each one is running from). Then send a simple message:
 Copy-paste prompt
hello If it responds, your brain is connected. That's your quick win.

 - (Optional but recommended) Enable computer use. This step tells Hermes: "this computer belongs to you." It makes everything Hermes does for you significantly simpler — you can just talk to it and it gets things done without you touching the machine. In your session, send:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, can you help me set up Hermes computer use? Hermes will start installing a package that lets it control and access your computer whenever it needs to do something for you.

 - Grant the macOS permissions. Hermes will ask for permissions — give it everything it needs, including the driver access permission it requests (follow the on-screen steps in the video). Hit Enable , then Quit and Restart when prompted. After the restart, Hermes Agent should be able to use your computer.
 Copy-paste prompts 

 Hey, can you help me set up Hermes computer use? Troubleshooting 

 Samin hits a real snag in the video: Hermes says computer-use setup isn't fully done, even though the permissions were already granted in macOS settings. The fix is simple — screenshot your permission settings and send the screenshot to Hermes in the chat. Tell it the permissions are turned on, let it look at the screenshot, and ask it to verify again. That basic loop — turn the setting on, screenshot it, hand it to the agent — solves most permission hiccups.

 ✅ Your Day 2 post 

 Post a screenshot of your first successful Hermes chat (your "hello" and its reply, with GPT 5.5 selected as the model) in the community — bonus points if you mention your backup model is configured too.

pages.title: Put Your Agent in Your Pocket (Telegram)
pages.folder: Day 03 — Put Your Agent in Your Pocket (Telegram)
course_path: Day 03 — Put Your Agent in Your Pocket (Telegram)
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Put Your Agent in Your Pocket (Telegram) What you'll have by the end: Hermes answering you on your phone through Telegram, plus a Topics group where Hermes has built its own separate threads — one for your course builds, one for trading, and one capture channel for dumping ideas.

 Why this matters: If Hermes can only talk to you at your desk, it can only work when you're at your desk. Channels come first because they're how you measure the three things that matter: work done while you're away, the percentage of your work that's automated, and revenue per (AI) employee.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps - Grab the setup guide (optional but helpful). In the free community, go to Classroom → YouTube video resources → Hermes Agent channel setup guide . It has the links and prompts used in this chapter, including a link that opens BotFather for you. The mural board and other resources are linked in the video description.
 - Open the Telegram settings in Hermes. In the Hermes desktop app, go to Messaging , then find Telegram . You'll see it needs a bot token from BotFather.
 - Create your bot with BotFather. Open BotFather in Telegram (use the link in the setup guide, then hit Start ). Type /newbot . Give it a name (Samin uses something like Hermes_course_video_bot ), then choose a username when it asks. BotFather will hand you a bot token .
 - Save the token in Hermes. Copy the token, paste it into the Telegram field in the Hermes desktop app, hit Save changes , then scroll down and hit Save . Hermes will restart — the yellow indicator means it's restarting. When it's done, the Telegram status should flip to connected .
 - Pair the bot. Back in Hermes, click the pairing link, then send /start to your new bot in Telegram. It gives you a pairing code . Copy it, open a new session in Hermes, paste the code, and use the pairing prompt below. Hermes will pair itself.
 - Say hi and set your home chat. Message your bot in Telegram — it should recognize you automatically and respond. When it asks, set that chat as your home chat . Any random updates Hermes needs to send you will land there.
 - Create a Topics group. In Telegram, make a new group (you don't need to add anyone). Call it something like YouTube course topic and hit Create .
 - Add your Hermes bot as an admin. Open your bot, add it to the group, then make it an admin . Admin gives it everything it needs. Say hi in the group to confirm the bot is in there.
 - Enable Topics. Click the group name, go to Edit → Topics , and enable topics . Samin sets the view to tabs . You'll now have a General chat, and you can add more tabs.
 - Let Hermes build its own threads. Instead of making topics by hand, ask Hermes to do it — use the "three topic threads" prompt below. While it works, you'll see "Hermes is typing" — that's one of Telegram's best features, because you can always tell when it's working, and you can hop between threads and run things simultaneously.
 - Open Telegram on your phone. This is the whole point — you'll see the same threads and updates on mobile, so Hermes works with you wherever you are. Once the threads are created, say hi in each one to test them.
 Copy-paste prompts Fix a stuck restart (send to Codex): 

 Hey, my Hermes agent is not working. It was trying to set up Telegram, but it just failed and it keeps restarting — but it's not restarting. Something's going on. Can you help fix it? Pair your Telegram bot (send to Hermes, paste your pairing code after it): 

 Hey, I'm trying to connect my Telegram bot. I just got this message, can you help me out? Create your topic threads (send to Hermes in your Topics group): 

 Hey, can you make three different topic threads? One of them can be for the builds for this YouTube course. Another one, make it for trading. And another one, just make a capture channel. Troubleshooting Hermes won't restart after saving the token. This happened live in the video. Use the buddy system: open a new chat in Codex and send the fix prompt above. Codex diagnoses it, and Telegram goes from nothing to connected . Expect a few snags like this early on — with the buddy system, any bump in the road gets fixed the same way.

 The bot can't create topics or won't respond in the group. Make sure all its admin permissions are turned on inside that group: click the group name → Edit → Admins , click your bot, and toggle everything on. Then tell Hermes to try again.

 Too much "reasoning" text in replies. Samin mentions he'll show how to turn that off later in the course — don't worry about it for now.

 ✅ Your Day 3 post Post a screenshot from your phone showing Hermes replying to you in Telegram — bonus points if your Topics group shows the three threads it created (course builds, trading, and capture).
pages.html:
🎬 Watch the video above, then follow the steps below.

Put Your Agent in Your Pocket (Telegram)

What you'll have by the end: Hermes answering you on your phone through Telegram, plus a Topics group where Hermes has built its own separate threads — one for your course builds, one for trading, and one capture channel for dumping ideas.

Why this matters: If Hermes can only talk to you at your desk, it can only work when you're at your desk. Channels come first because they're how you measure the three things that matter: work done while you're away, the percentage of your work that's automated, and revenue per (AI) employee.

Watch the video, then follow these steps

  1. Grab the setup guide (optional but helpful). In the free community, go to Classroom → YouTube video resources → Hermes Agent channel setup guide. It has the links and prompts used in this chapter, including a link that opens BotFather for you. The mural board and other resources are linked in the video description.
  1. Open the Telegram settings in Hermes. In the Hermes desktop app, go to Messaging, then find Telegram. You'll see it needs a bot token from BotFather.
  1. Create your bot with BotFather. Open BotFather in Telegram (use the link in the setup guide, then hit Start). Type /newbot. Give it a name (Samin uses something like Hermes_course_video_bot), then choose a username when it asks. BotFather will hand you a bot token.
  1. Save the token in Hermes. Copy the token, paste it into the Telegram field in the Hermes desktop app, hit Save changes, then scroll down and hit Save. Hermes will restart — the yellow indicator means it's restarting. When it's done, the Telegram status should flip to connected.
  1. Pair the bot. Back in Hermes, click the pairing link, then send /start to your new bot in Telegram. It gives you a pairing code. Copy it, open a new session in Hermes, paste the code, and use the pairing prompt below. Hermes will pair itself.
  1. Say hi and set your home chat. Message your bot in Telegram — it should recognize you automatically and respond. When it asks, set that chat as your home chat. Any random updates Hermes needs to send you will land there.
  1. Create a Topics group. In Telegram, make a new group (you don't need to add anyone). Call it something like YouTube course topic and hit Create.
  1. Add your Hermes bot as an admin. Open your bot, add it to the group, then make it an admin. Admin gives it everything it needs. Say hi in the group to confirm the bot is in there.
  1. Enable Topics. Click the group name, go to Edit → Topics, and enable topics. Samin sets the view to tabs. You'll now have a General chat, and you can add more tabs.
  1. Let Hermes build its own threads. Instead of making topics by hand, ask Hermes to do it — use the "three topic threads" prompt below. While it works, you'll see "Hermes is typing" — that's one of Telegram's best features, because you can always tell when it's working, and you can hop between threads and run things simultaneously.
  1. Open Telegram on your phone. This is the whole point — you'll see the same threads and updates on mobile, so Hermes works with you wherever you are. Once the threads are created, say hi in each one to test them.

Copy-paste prompts

Fix a stuck restart (send to Codex):

Hey, my Hermes agent is not working. It was trying to set up Telegram, but it just failed and it keeps restarting — but it's not restarting. Something's going on. Can you help fix it?

Pair your Telegram bot (send to Hermes, paste your pairing code after it):

Hey, I'm trying to connect my Telegram bot. I just got this message, can you help me out?

Create your topic threads (send to Hermes in your Topics group):

Hey, can you make three different topic threads? One of them can be for the builds for this YouTube course. Another one, make it for trading. And another one, just make a capture channel.

Troubleshooting

Hermes won't restart after saving the token. This happened live in the video. Use the buddy system: open a new chat in Codex and send the fix prompt above. Codex diagnoses it, and Telegram goes from nothing to connected. Expect a few snags like this early on — with the buddy system, any bump in the road gets fixed the same way.

The bot can't create topics or won't respond in the group. Make sure all its admin permissions are turned on inside that group: click the group name → Edit → Admins, click your bot, and toggle everything on. Then tell Hermes to try again.

Too much "reasoning" text in replies. Samin mentions he'll show how to turn that off later in the course — don't worry about it for now.

✅ Your Day 3 post

Post a screenshot from your phone showing Hermes replying to you in Telegram — bonus points if your Topics group shows the three threads it created (course builds, trading, and capture).

rag.search_text:
Put Your Agent in Your Pocket (Telegram)

Day 03 — Put Your Agent in Your Pocket (Telegram)

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Put Your Agent in Your Pocket (Telegram) What you'll have by the end: Hermes answering you on your phone through Telegram, plus a Topics group where Hermes has built its own separate threads — one for your course builds, one for trading, and one capture channel for dumping ideas.

 Why this matters: If Hermes can only talk to you at your desk, it can only work when you're at your desk. Channels come first because they're how you measure the three things that matter: work done while you're away, the percentage of your work that's automated, and revenue per (AI) employee.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps - Grab the setup guide (optional but helpful). In the free community, go to Classroom → YouTube video resources → Hermes Agent channel setup guide . It has the links and prompts used in this chapter, including a link that opens BotFather for you. The mural board and other resources are linked in the video description.
 - Open the Telegram settings in Hermes. In the Hermes desktop app, go to Messaging , then find Telegram . You'll see it needs a bot token from BotFather.
 - Create your bot with BotFather. Open BotFather in Telegram (use the link in the setup guide, then hit Start ). Type /newbot . Give it a name (Samin uses something like Hermes_course_video_bot ), then choose a username when it asks. BotFather will hand you a bot token .
 - Save the token in Hermes. Copy the token, paste it into the Telegram field in the Hermes desktop app, hit Save changes , then scroll down and hit Save . Hermes will restart — the yellow indicator means it's restarting. When it's done, the Telegram status should flip to connected .
 - Pair the bot. Back in Hermes, click the pairing link, then send /start to your new bot in Telegram. It gives you a pairing code . Copy it, open a new session in Hermes, paste the code, and use the pairing prompt below. Hermes will pair itself.
 - Say hi and set your home chat. Message your bot in Telegram — it should recognize you automatically and respond. When it asks, set that chat as your home chat . Any random updates Hermes needs to send you will land there.
 - Create a Topics group. In Telegram, make a new group (you don't need to add anyone). Call it something like YouTube course topic and hit Create .
 - Add your Hermes bot as an admin. Open your bot, add it to the group, then make it an admin . Admin gives it everything it needs. Say hi in the group to confirm the bot is in there.
 - Enable Topics. Click the group name, go to Edit → Topics , and enable topics . Samin sets the view to tabs . You'll now have a General chat, and you can add more tabs.
 - Let Hermes build its own threads. Instead of making topics by hand, ask Hermes to do it — use the "three topic threads" prompt below. While it works, you'll see "Hermes is typing" — that's one of Telegram's best features, because you can always tell when it's working, and you can hop between threads and run things simultaneously.
 - Open Telegram on your phone. This is the whole point — you'll see the same threads and updates on mobile, so Hermes works with you wherever you are. Once the threads are created, say hi in each one to test them.
 Copy-paste prompts Fix a stuck restart (send to Codex): 

 Hey, my Hermes agent is not working. It was trying to set up Telegram, but it just failed and it keeps restarting — but it's not restarting. Something's going on. Can you help fix it? Pair your Telegram bot (send to Hermes, paste your pairing code after it): 

 Hey, I'm trying to connect my Telegram bot. I just got this message, can you help me out? Create your topic threads (send to Hermes in your Topics group): 

 Hey, can you make three different topic threads? One of them can be for the builds for this YouTube course. Another one, make it for trading. And another one, just make a capture channel. Troubleshooting Hermes won't restart after saving the token. This happened live in the video. Use the buddy system: open a new chat in Codex and send the fix prompt above. Codex diagnoses it, and Telegram goes from nothing to connected . Expect a few snags like this early on — with the buddy system, any bump in the road gets fixed the same way.

 The bot can't create topics or won't respond in the group. Make sure all its admin permissions are turned on inside that group: click the group name → Edit → Admins , click your bot, and toggle everything on. Then tell Hermes to try again.

 Too much "reasoning" text in replies. Samin mentions he'll show how to turn that off later in the course — don't worry about it for now.

 ✅ Your Day 3 post Post a screenshot from your phone showing Hermes replying to you in Telegram — bonus points if your Topics group shows the three threads it created (course builds, trading, and capture).

pages.title: Bring Your Agent Into Slack & iMessage
pages.folder: Day 04 — Bring Your Agent Into Slack & iMessage
course_path: Day 04 — Bring Your Agent Into Slack & iMessage
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Bring Your Agent Into Slack & iMessage What you'll have by the end 

 Hermes replying cleanly inside Slack channels and threads (with teammates whitelisted to use it), plus its own iMessage number that answers texts straight from your phone.

 Why this matters 

 Slack turns Hermes into an actual teammate — one shared brain your whole team can talk to, so nothing stays siloed. And iMessage gives you the fastest possible way to fire off tasks: just text it.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 

 Part 1: Slack 

 - In your Hermes dashboard, go to Messaging and find Slack . You'll need two things: a bot token and an app token . Click the button next to the Slack setup to open Slack's app creation page.
 - Choose Create a new app from scratch . Name it something like Hermes YouTube course , pick your workspace (Samin uses his "AI Answers" workspace), and create it.
 - Get the bot token. Go to OAuth & Permissions , scroll down to scopes, and add these three: commands , im:write , and im:read . This is the minimum — you can add more scopes later if you want Hermes to do more in Slack.
 - Still on OAuth & Permissions, hit Install to [your workspace] and click Allow . Copy the bot token it gives you.
 - Go back to Hermes and paste the bot token into the Slack bot token field — but don't hit save yet .
 - Back in your Slack app settings, go to Socket Mode and turn it on. Give the token a name (Samin calls his "U2"), hit Generate , and copy it.
 - Paste that into the Slack app token field in Hermes. Now hit Save changes .
 - Restart the gateway. The gateway is what lets Hermes talk to all your channels — any time you add or remove a channel, restart it. If it acts up, just close it and start it again.
 - Now install the bot inside Slack itself. In your Slack app settings, go to App Home and make sure "Always show my bot as online" is on, and check the messaging options below it.
 - In Slack, search for your bot by name ( Hermes YouTube course ), open a DM with it, and say hi. If it doesn't respond, follow the on-screen troubleshooting in the video.
 - Test it in a channel. Create a new channel (Samin makes a private one called Hermes YouTube Test ), add the bot to the channel, and message it. You'll see it replies in threads.
 - If Hermes clutters its replies with extra status text, just tell it:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, I'm not trying to see all these useless texts — just send me your response. That's how I want to see responses in the future. It saves that to memory, and future replies come back clean.

 - Whitelist your teammates. Two ways: in your Hermes Slack integration settings, add their Slack IDs (or set allow all users = true), or just tell Hermes in Slack: "Hey, whitelist this user." That's it — they can now talk to your bot.
 Part 2: iMessage via Photon 

 - Go to photon.codes and sign up — it's free to get started. It'll ask for your phone number.
 - Create a project. Call it Hermes YouTube course , pick iMessage , and create it. You'll see the iMessage number you'll be texting.
 - Scroll down to Photon's docs. Here's the payoff of giving Hermes computer control: instead of configuring this yourself, copy the docs (and your Photon credentials), paste them into Hermes, and let it do the setup:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, I want you to use these docs to set up my Hermes gateway so I can be messaging it in iMessage. Here are my credentials. Can you set this all up? Let me know when it's done. - Hit enter and let it work. Since Hermes already has the skills from setting up Slack and Telegram, this goes fast.
 - While it works, go back to Photon and scan the QR code — that's what enables texting the number.
 - When Hermes says done ("Photon iMessage configured, gateway is running"), refresh the window, grab your phone, and text "Hi" to your Photon number. Hermes texts back.
 Copy-paste prompts 

 Clean Slack replies:

 Hey, I'm not trying to see all these useless texts — just send me your response. That's how I want to see responses in the future. Whitelist a teammate (say this to Hermes in Slack):

 Hey, whitelist this user. Have Hermes configure iMessage itself (paste Photon's docs + your credentials first):

 Hey, I want you to use these docs to set up my Hermes gateway so I can be messaging it in iMessage. Here are my credentials. Can you set this all up? Let me know when it's done. Troubleshooting 

 If the gateway doesn't come back up after you save your Slack tokens, just close it and start it again — a fresh restart fixes it. And if the bot doesn't reply when you first message it in Slack, follow the on-screen troubleshooting steps in the video.

 ✅ Your Day 4 post 

 Post a screenshot of Hermes replying in a Slack thread, or of it answering your "Hi" text over iMessage — either one counts as your Day 4 win.
pages.html:
🎬 Watch the video above, then follow the steps below.

Bring Your Agent Into Slack & iMessage

What you'll have by the end

Hermes replying cleanly inside Slack channels and threads (with teammates whitelisted to use it), plus its own iMessage number that answers texts straight from your phone.

Why this matters

Slack turns Hermes into an actual teammate — one shared brain your whole team can talk to, so nothing stays siloed. And iMessage gives you the fastest possible way to fire off tasks: just text it.

Watch the video, then follow these steps

Part 1: Slack

  1. In your Hermes dashboard, go to Messaging and find Slack. You'll need two things: a bot token and an app token. Click the button next to the Slack setup to open Slack's app creation page.
  1. Choose Create a new app from scratch. Name it something like Hermes YouTube course, pick your workspace (Samin uses his "AI Answers" workspace), and create it.
  1. Get the bot token. Go to OAuth & Permissions, scroll down to scopes, and add these three: commands, im:write, and im:read. This is the minimum — you can add more scopes later if you want Hermes to do more in Slack.
  1. Still on OAuth & Permissions, hit Install to [your workspace] and click Allow. Copy the bot token it gives you.
  1. Go back to Hermes and paste the bot token into the Slack bot token field — but don't hit save yet.
  1. Back in your Slack app settings, go to Socket Mode and turn it on. Give the token a name (Samin calls his "U2"), hit Generate, and copy it.
  1. Paste that into the Slack app token field in Hermes. Now hit Save changes.
  1. Restart the gateway. The gateway is what lets Hermes talk to all your channels — any time you add or remove a channel, restart it. If it acts up, just close it and start it again.
  1. Now install the bot inside Slack itself. In your Slack app settings, go to App Home and make sure "Always show my bot as online" is on, and check the messaging options below it.
  1. In Slack, search for your bot by name (Hermes YouTube course), open a DM with it, and say hi. If it doesn't respond, follow the on-screen troubleshooting in the video.
  1. Test it in a channel. Create a new channel (Samin makes a private one called Hermes YouTube Test), add the bot to the channel, and message it. You'll see it replies in threads.
  1. If Hermes clutters its replies with extra status text, just tell it:
Copy-paste prompt
Hey, I'm not trying to see all these useless texts — just send me your response. That's how I want to see responses in the future.

It saves that to memory, and future replies come back clean.

  1. Whitelist your teammates. Two ways: in your Hermes Slack integration settings, add their Slack IDs (or set allow all users = true), or just tell Hermes in Slack: "Hey, whitelist this user." That's it — they can now talk to your bot.

Part 2: iMessage via Photon

  1. Go to photon.codes and sign up — it's free to get started. It'll ask for your phone number.
  1. Create a project. Call it Hermes YouTube course, pick iMessage, and create it. You'll see the iMessage number you'll be texting.
  1. Scroll down to Photon's docs. Here's the payoff of giving Hermes computer control: instead of configuring this yourself, copy the docs (and your Photon credentials), paste them into Hermes, and let it do the setup:
Copy-paste prompt
Hey, I want you to use these docs to set up my Hermes gateway so I can be messaging it in iMessage. Here are my credentials. Can you set this all up? Let me know when it's done.
  1. Hit enter and let it work. Since Hermes already has the skills from setting up Slack and Telegram, this goes fast.
  1. While it works, go back to Photon and scan the QR code — that's what enables texting the number.
  1. When Hermes says done ("Photon iMessage configured, gateway is running"), refresh the window, grab your phone, and text "Hi" to your Photon number. Hermes texts back.

Copy-paste prompts

Clean Slack replies:

Hey, I'm not trying to see all these useless texts — just send me your response. That's how I want to see responses in the future.

Whitelist a teammate (say this to Hermes in Slack):

Hey, whitelist this user.

Have Hermes configure iMessage itself (paste Photon's docs + your credentials first):

Hey, I want you to use these docs to set up my Hermes gateway so I can be messaging it in iMessage. Here are my credentials. Can you set this all up? Let me know when it's done.

Troubleshooting

If the gateway doesn't come back up after you save your Slack tokens, just close it and start it again — a fresh restart fixes it. And if the bot doesn't reply when you first message it in Slack, follow the on-screen troubleshooting steps in the video.

✅ Your Day 4 post

Post a screenshot of Hermes replying in a Slack thread, or of it answering your "Hi" text over iMessage — either one counts as your Day 4 win.

rag.search_text:
Bring Your Agent Into Slack & iMessage

Day 04 — Bring Your Agent Into Slack & iMessage

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Bring Your Agent Into Slack & iMessage What you'll have by the end 

 Hermes replying cleanly inside Slack channels and threads (with teammates whitelisted to use it), plus its own iMessage number that answers texts straight from your phone.

 Why this matters 

 Slack turns Hermes into an actual teammate — one shared brain your whole team can talk to, so nothing stays siloed. And iMessage gives you the fastest possible way to fire off tasks: just text it.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 

 Part 1: Slack 

 - In your Hermes dashboard, go to Messaging and find Slack . You'll need two things: a bot token and an app token . Click the button next to the Slack setup to open Slack's app creation page.
 - Choose Create a new app from scratch . Name it something like Hermes YouTube course , pick your workspace (Samin uses his "AI Answers" workspace), and create it.
 - Get the bot token. Go to OAuth & Permissions , scroll down to scopes, and add these three: commands , im:write , and im:read . This is the minimum — you can add more scopes later if you want Hermes to do more in Slack.
 - Still on OAuth & Permissions, hit Install to [your workspace] and click Allow . Copy the bot token it gives you.
 - Go back to Hermes and paste the bot token into the Slack bot token field — but don't hit save yet .
 - Back in your Slack app settings, go to Socket Mode and turn it on. Give the token a name (Samin calls his "U2"), hit Generate , and copy it.
 - Paste that into the Slack app token field in Hermes. Now hit Save changes .
 - Restart the gateway. The gateway is what lets Hermes talk to all your channels — any time you add or remove a channel, restart it. If it acts up, just close it and start it again.
 - Now install the bot inside Slack itself. In your Slack app settings, go to App Home and make sure "Always show my bot as online" is on, and check the messaging options below it.
 - In Slack, search for your bot by name ( Hermes YouTube course ), open a DM with it, and say hi. If it doesn't respond, follow the on-screen troubleshooting in the video.
 - Test it in a channel. Create a new channel (Samin makes a private one called Hermes YouTube Test ), add the bot to the channel, and message it. You'll see it replies in threads.
 - If Hermes clutters its replies with extra status text, just tell it:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, I'm not trying to see all these useless texts — just send me your response. That's how I want to see responses in the future. It saves that to memory, and future replies come back clean.

 - Whitelist your teammates. Two ways: in your Hermes Slack integration settings, add their Slack IDs (or set allow all users = true), or just tell Hermes in Slack: "Hey, whitelist this user." That's it — they can now talk to your bot.
 Part 2: iMessage via Photon 

 - Go to photon.codes and sign up — it's free to get started. It'll ask for your phone number.
 - Create a project. Call it Hermes YouTube course , pick iMessage , and create it. You'll see the iMessage number you'll be texting.
 - Scroll down to Photon's docs. Here's the payoff of giving Hermes computer control: instead of configuring this yourself, copy the docs (and your Photon credentials), paste them into Hermes, and let it do the setup:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, I want you to use these docs to set up my Hermes gateway so I can be messaging it in iMessage. Here are my credentials. Can you set this all up? Let me know when it's done. - Hit enter and let it work. Since Hermes already has the skills from setting up Slack and Telegram, this goes fast.
 - While it works, go back to Photon and scan the QR code — that's what enables texting the number.
 - When Hermes says done ("Photon iMessage configured, gateway is running"), refresh the window, grab your phone, and text "Hi" to your Photon number. Hermes texts back.
 Copy-paste prompts 

 Clean Slack replies:

 Hey, I'm not trying to see all these useless texts — just send me your response. That's how I want to see responses in the future. Whitelist a teammate (say this to Hermes in Slack):

 Hey, whitelist this user. Have Hermes configure iMessage itself (paste Photon's docs + your credentials first):

 Hey, I want you to use these docs to set up my Hermes gateway so I can be messaging it in iMessage. Here are my credentials. Can you set this all up? Let me know when it's done. Troubleshooting 

 If the gateway doesn't come back up after you save your Slack tokens, just close it and start it again — a fresh restart fixes it. And if the bot doesn't reply when you first message it in Slack, follow the on-screen troubleshooting steps in the video.

 ✅ Your Day 4 post 

 Post a screenshot of Hermes replying in a Slack thread, or of it answering your "Hi" text over iMessage — either one counts as your Day 4 win.

pages.title: Give Your Agent Your Personality
pages.folder: Day 05 — Personality & Memory
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Give Your Agent Your Personality What you'll have by the end: A customized soul.md so Hermes talks the way you want, and a populated user.md so it actually remembers who you are across days and weeks — a real assistant, not a generic chatbot.

 Why this matters: You'll be talking to Hermes every day, on every channel. If it drones on for pages of ChatGPT-style filler and forgets you between sessions, you won't use it. Two small files fix both problems.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 1. Understand what `soul.md` does. Hermes's personality lives in a file called soul.md . It gets loaded every single time Hermes talks to you, and it tells Hermes how to speak, what tone to use, and what its purpose is. It also evolves over time if you ask it to change.

 2. See what a good soul looks like. In the video, Samin shows a real conversation with his Hermes: it gives him his flight info with "Airport Goblin Tax included," tells him when to leave to maximize "lounge goblin mode," talks in lowercase, keeps it short, and drops quick links inline — no pages of useless filler. That's the soul file at work: fun to talk to, remembers his jokes, gets to the point.

 3. Find your soul file. Open your Hermes agent. On the bottom left, you'll see it says default . Click Manage Profiles — this is where the soul lives. You'll see the default soul for your Hermes agent. You technically don't have to touch it at all, but if you want Hermes to talk differently, keep going.

 4. Grab the identity files board. Samin's whole board of identity files is linked in the video description and in the free community. It contains the interview questions and his personal soul.md .

 5. Option A — the interview (recommended). On the board, find the interview questions — a long list of specific questions so Hermes can get to know you. Copy them, go back to your Hermes, paste them in, and add this:

 Copy-paste prompt

[Paste the interview questions from the board here]

Interview me. Then just go back and forth answering. By the end, Hermes knows all your details.

 6. Option B — use Samin's proven soul (faster). If you'd rather skip the interview and just use how Samin likes talking to Hermes: copy his soul.md from the board, go to Manage Profiles , paste it in, and save your soul.md . His soul is built around a few rules: get to the point, lead with answers, have actual opinions, and back them with evidence. Once saved, your Hermes immediately acts differently — and better.

 7. Populate your `user.md`. The second identity file is user.md — the file where Hermes writes down everything it learns about you. This is why the interview matters: after you've answered all the questions, tell Hermes to store it:

 Copy-paste prompt

Remember all this stuff about me. Hermes writes it into user.md , and from then on it remembers you across days and weeks — it feels like working with an assistant instead of a generic chat box.

 8. Test it. Ask Hermes something and notice the difference in tone. Then ask it something about you — it should already know the answer.

 A quick note on profiles: a profile is just one Hermes personality and bot, with all its connections. Samin covers profiles in more depth later in the course.

 Copy-paste prompts Start the interview (paste the questions from the board first):

 Interview me. Save everything to user.md (after answering the questions):

 Remember all this stuff about me. ✅ Your Day 5 post Post a screenshot of Hermes replying in your new personality (or a before/after of the same question) — bonus points if it already remembers a detail about you from your interview.

pages.folder_normalized: Day 05 — Personality & Memory
pages.location_label: Hermes Agent Course [BETA] → Day 05 — Personality & Memory → Give Your Agent Your Personality

rag.search_text:
Give Your Agent Your Personality

Day 05 — Personality & Memory

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Give Your Agent Your Personality What you'll have by the end: A customized soul.md so Hermes talks the way you want, and a populated user.md so it actually remembers who you are across days and weeks — a real assistant, not a generic chatbot.

 Why this matters: You'll be talking to Hermes every day, on every channel. If it drones on for pages of ChatGPT-style filler and forgets you between sessions, you won't use it. Two small files fix both problems.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 1. Understand what `soul.md` does. Hermes's personality lives in a file called soul.md . It gets loaded every single time Hermes talks to you, and it tells Hermes how to speak, what tone to use, and what its purpose is. It also evolves over time if you ask it to change.

 2. See what a good soul looks like. In the video, Samin shows a real conversation with his Hermes: it gives him his flight info with "Airport Goblin Tax included," tells him when to leave to maximize "lounge goblin mode," talks in lowercase, keeps it short, and drops quick links inline — no pages of useless filler. That's the soul file at work: fun to talk to, remembers his jokes, gets to the point.

 3. Find your soul file. Open your Hermes agent. On the bottom left, you'll see it says default . Click Manage Profiles — this is where the soul lives. You'll see the default soul for your Hermes agent. You technically don't have to touch it at all, but if you want Hermes to talk differently, keep going.

 4. Grab the identity files board. Samin's whole board of identity files is linked in the video description and in the free community. It contains the interview questions and his personal soul.md .

 5. Option A — the interview (recommended). On the board, find the interview questions — a long list of specific questions so Hermes can get to know you. Copy them, go back to your Hermes, paste them in, and add this:

 Copy-paste prompt

[Paste the interview questions from the board here]

Interview me. Then just go back and forth answering. By the end, Hermes knows all your details.

 6. Option B — use Samin's proven soul (faster). If you'd rather skip the interview and just use how Samin likes talking to Hermes: copy his soul.md from the board, go to Manage Profiles , paste it in, and save your soul.md . His soul is built around a few rules: get to the point, lead with answers, have actual opinions, and back them with evidence. Once saved, your Hermes immediately acts differently — and better.

 7. Populate your `user.md`. The second identity file is user.md — the file where Hermes writes down everything it learns about you. This is why the interview matters: after you've answered all the questions, tell Hermes to store it:

 Copy-paste prompt

Remember all this stuff about me. Hermes writes it into user.md , and from then on it remembers you across days and weeks — it feels like working with an assistant instead of a generic chat box.

 8. Test it. Ask Hermes something and notice the difference in tone. Then ask it something about you — it should already know the answer.

 A quick note on profiles: a profile is just one Hermes personality and bot, with all its connections. Samin covers profiles in more depth later in the course.

 Copy-paste prompts Start the interview (paste the questions from the board first):

 Interview me. Save everything to user.md (after answering the questions):

 Remember all this stuff about me. ✅ Your Day 5 post Post a screenshot of Hermes replying in your new personality (or a before/after of the same question) — bonus points if it already remembers a detail about you from your interview.

pages.title: Give Your Agent a Real Memory
pages.folder: Day 05 — Personality & Memory
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Give Your Agent a Real Memory What you'll have by the end 

 Honcho connected as your Hermes memory provider via a one-click OAuth login. Your agent now has persistent, long-term memory across every chat, every channel, and every tool you use.

 Why this matters 

 Memory is what lets your agent stop acting like a new intern every time you open a chat. This is the setup that makes Hermes + Honcho work ten times better than Claude Code or Codex alone.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 

 The setup itself is at the end and takes about a minute. But watch the first part of the video too — understanding *why* memory is hard is what makes the rest of this course click.

 - Understand what "basic" memory is. Most memory products (including the built-in Hermes memory, and what's baked into Claude Code and Codex) can remember a fact you said and bring it up later. That's table stakes. Real agent memory is harder, and it breaks down into four problems.
 - Problem 1 — single-session memory. In one long conversation, the agent has to keep following instructions you gave 40 minutes ago (like "keep this direct, no fluff") and remember its own work — files it created, plans it made, things it promised to follow up on.
 - Problem 2 — knowledge updates. If you change jobs, the agent shouldn't keep talking about your old one. If you used to like long explanations but now keep asking for direct scripts, good memory updates — it doesn't treat old facts as equally true as new ones.
 - Problem 3 — multi-session memory. Useful info gets spread across conversations: you plan a course in one chat, make visuals in another, debug the setup in Claude Code later. A real memory system connects all those pieces. Otherwise every new session feels like starting from scratch.
 - Problem 4 — temporal reasoning. The hardest one. The agent needs to understand *time*: what happened last year vs. yesterday, and which preference matters right now. Not just "what did the user say" but "when did they say it, is it still true, does a new fact replace it?"
 - Meet Honcho. Honcho is one of the fastest-growing open-source projects, and it solves the cold-start problem: with Honcho installed, it becomes your memory router. Doesn't matter which chat you open — it always has context.
 - One shared brain. This is the key idea from the diagram in the video: whether you're using Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Hermes, or OpenClaw, everything flows into one shared brain that grows over time. You get to use whatever tool is best for the job without losing context.
 - Peer cards. Honcho builds a "peer card" for everyone in your life — you, your manager, your coworker Bob, even your AI agents. It gleans these from your messages automatically: Bob can't code, your manager thinks you're hardworking, and so on. In the video, Samin shows his own Honcho with tons of sessions and peer cards you can search through.
 - Dreaming. Just like you sleep on new information and internalize it, Honcho takes raw chats and reasons over them to create useful memory. It does this through Neuromancer , a fine-tuned model (you can use any AI model for the reasoning). This is where knowledge updates, logical implications, contradictions, and peer cards get processed.
 - A word of caution. Honcho is powerful, which makes it dangerous in the wrong hands — you're putting details from everywhere into one memory system. The escape hatch: it's open source, so you can self-host it on a Mac Mini (or wherever you want). Then nobody has access to it but you, and you still get a great, private system.
 - Now connect it. Honcho comes pre-packaged with your Hermes Agent. In Hermes, go to Settings → Memory and Context . Under Memory provider , choose Honcho .
 - Click "Connect via OAuth." Make a Honcho account if you don't have one, enter your name, click Authorize , and that's it — you're connected. Follow the on-screen steps in the video if anything looks different.
 Copy-paste prompts 

 None this chapter — this one is all clicks, no prompts.

 ✅ Your Day 5 post 

 Post a screenshot of your Hermes Settings → Memory and Context page showing Honcho selected as your memory provider and connected via OAuth.
