Watch the video, then follow these steps

The setup itself is at the end and takes about a minute. But watch the first part of the video too — understanding *why* memory is hard is what makes the rest of this course click.

  1. Understand what "basic" memory is. Most memory products (including the built-in Hermes memory, and what's baked into Claude Code and Codex) can remember a fact you said and bring it up later. That's table stakes. Real agent memory is harder, and it breaks down into four problems.
  1. Problem 1 — single-session memory. In one long conversation, the agent has to keep following instructions you gave 40 minutes ago (like "keep this direct, no fluff") and remember its own work — files it created, plans it made, things it promised to follow up on.
  1. Problem 2 — knowledge updates. If you change jobs, the agent shouldn't keep talking about your old one. If you used to like long explanations but now keep asking for direct scripts, good memory updates — it doesn't treat old facts as equally true as new ones.
  1. Problem 3 — multi-session memory. Useful info gets spread across conversations: you plan a course in one chat, make visuals in another, debug the setup in Claude Code later. A real memory system connects all those pieces. Otherwise every new session feels like starting from scratch.
  1. Problem 4 — temporal reasoning. The hardest one. The agent needs to understand *time*: what happened last year vs. yesterday, and which preference matters right now. Not just "what did the user say" but "when did they say it, is it still true, does a new fact replace it?"
  1. Meet Honcho. Honcho is one of the fastest-growing open-source projects, and it solves the cold-start problem: with Honcho installed, it becomes your memory router. Doesn't matter which chat you open — it always has context.
  1. One shared brain. This is the key idea from the diagram in the video: whether you're using Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Hermes, or OpenClaw, everything flows into one shared brain that grows over time. You get to use whatever tool is best for the job without losing context.
  1. Peer cards. Honcho builds a "peer card" for everyone in your life — you, your manager, your coworker Bob, even your AI agents. It gleans these from your messages automatically: Bob can't code, your manager thinks you're hardworking, and so on. In the video, Samin shows his own Honcho with tons of sessions and peer cards you can search through.
  1. Dreaming. Just like you sleep on new information and internalize it, Honcho takes raw chats and reasons over them to create useful memory. It does this through Neuromancer, a fine-tuned model (you can use any AI model for the reasoning). This is where knowledge updates, logical implications, contradictions, and peer cards get processed.
  1. A word of caution. Honcho is powerful, which makes it dangerous in the wrong hands — you're putting details from everywhere into one memory system. The escape hatch: it's open source, so you can self-host it on a Mac Mini (or wherever you want). Then nobody has access to it but you, and you still get a great, private system.
  1. Now connect it. Honcho comes pre-packaged with your Hermes Agent. In Hermes, go to Settings → Memory and Context. Under Memory provider, choose Honcho.
  1. Click "Connect via OAuth." Make a Honcho account if you don't have one, enter your name, click Authorize, and that's it — you're connected. Follow the on-screen steps in the video if anything looks different.

Copy-paste prompts

None this chapter — this one is all clicks, no prompts.

✅ Your Day 5 post

Post a screenshot of your Hermes Settings → Memory and Context page showing Honcho selected as your memory provider and connected via OAuth.

transcript.transcript_text: thing I wanted to cover and that is the soul of the Hermes and then I want to take you through how the user MD works and the user MD is basically an identity tag. So this is a file where Hermes writes all this stuff about you. All right? And that's why I told you to answer all these questions and put it into Hermes. All you need to do is answer all these questions. You put it inside Hermes and just say remember all this stuff and then your Hermes basically just remembers you across days and weeks and it's not just a generic chatbot, right? And it feels more like working with an assistant and I really enjoy doing that. So the easiest way again is just to make the agent interview you. And if you can start from blank, it becomes really easy to use. All right, so just answer all those questions, dump it in, and you should be good to go. But after that's done, let's get to the fun part because now I want to show you what the memory is like and different options of memories you can use for your Hermes such that it's actually intelligent over time. So this part is super important. All right. So let me tell you why this is actually not a very simple problem. Because once you understand this, you can see why what we're doing matters so much and why Hermes is so impactful with the right setup that I'm going to show you, right? Because most people think memory is simple. It's just that the assistant should remember what you said and bring it up later. That's the basic version. Like if I tell an assistant I'm working on a Hermes course, it should remember that in a later or in the same conversation or in some other random conversation, right? This is just a low table stakes. Most memory products, even the in-built Hermes memory can do that and it's built into everything like cloud code, codecs, everything. But real agentic memory is harder than that. And let me describe why. The first problem is single session memory. So imagine there's a long conversation you're having and the assistant has to remember what the user said earlier. So if I say in the conversation, keep this direct, no fluff, and then we talk for let's say 40 more minutes, the assistant should still follow that preference, right? It has to also remember what the assistant did. If the assistant created the file, made a plan, or promised to follow up on something, it should not lose track of its own work. The second problem is knowledge updates. And this is where memory starts getting a little tricky. Let's say I ask the assistant about my job. It should not talk about the job that I had before in the past. It should know that my life has changed. Same with preferences. Maybe last month I liked long explanations. Now I keep saying give me direct scripts. Good memory should update and it should not treat old facts equally as true as new facts. And the third problem is multi-session memory. So this is where useful information is spread across different conversations. So if I'm having a conversation in Telegram, Slack, maybe let's say I planned the Hermes course in one chat, created a visuals in another one, and then debug the setup in cloud code later, right? A good memory system has to connect all those different moving pieces together. Otherwise, every new session feels like I'm starting over from scratch. The fourth problem is temporal reasoning. rag.search_text:
Give Your Agent a Real Memory

Day 05 — Personality & Memory

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Give Your Agent a Real Memory What you'll have by the end 

 Honcho connected as your Hermes memory provider via a one-click OAuth login. Your agent now has persistent, long-term memory across every chat, every channel, and every tool you use.

 Why this matters 

 Memory is what lets your agent stop acting like a new intern every time you open a chat. This is the setup that makes Hermes + Honcho work ten times better than Claude Code or Codex alone.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 

 The setup itself is at the end and takes about a minute. But watch the first part of the video too — understanding *why* memory is hard is what makes the rest of this course click.

 - Understand what "basic" memory is. Most memory products (including the built-in Hermes memory, and what's baked into Claude Code and Codex) can remember a fact you said and bring it up later. That's table stakes. Real agent memory is harder, and it breaks down into four problems.
 - Problem 1 — single-session memory. In one long conversation, the agent has to keep following instructions you gave 40 minutes ago (like "keep this direct, no fluff") and remember its own work — files it created, plans it made, things it promised to follow up on.
 - Problem 2 — knowledge updates. If you change jobs, the agent shouldn't keep talking about your old one. If you used to like long explanations but now keep asking for direct scripts, good memory updates — it doesn't treat old facts as equally true as new ones.
 - Problem 3 — multi-session memory. Useful info gets spread across conversations: you plan a course in one chat, make visuals in another, debug the setup in Claude Code later. A real memory system connects all those pieces. Otherwise every new session feels like starting from scratch.
 - Problem 4 — temporal reasoning. The hardest one. The agent needs to understand *time*: what happened last year vs. yesterday, and which preference matters right now. Not just "what did the user say" but "when did they say it, is it still true, does a new fact replace it?"
 - Meet Honcho. Honcho is one of the fastest-growing open-source projects, and it solves the cold-start problem: with Honcho installed, it becomes your memory router. Doesn't matter which chat you open — it always has context.
 - One shared brain. This is the key idea from the diagram in the video: whether you're using Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Hermes, or OpenClaw, everything flows into one shared brain that grows over time. You get to use whatever tool is best for the job without losing context.
 - Peer cards. Honcho builds a "peer card" for everyone in your life — you, your manager, your coworker Bob, even your AI agents. It gleans these from your messages automatically: Bob can't code, your manager thinks you're hardworking, and so on. In the video, Samin shows his own Honcho with tons of sessions and peer cards you can search through.
 - Dreaming. Just like you sleep on new information and internalize it, Honcho takes raw chats and reasons over them to create useful memory. It does this through Neuromancer , a fine-tuned model (you can use any AI model for the reasoning). This is where knowledge updates, logical implications, contradictions, and peer cards get processed.
 - A word of caution. Honcho is powerful, which makes it dangerous in the wrong hands — you're putting details from everywhere into one memory system. The escape hatch: it's open source, so you can self-host it on a Mac Mini (or wherever you want). Then nobody has access to it but you, and you still get a great, private system.
 - Now connect it. Honcho comes pre-packaged with your Hermes Agent. In Hermes, go to Settings → Memory and Context . Under Memory provider , choose Honcho .
 - Click "Connect via OAuth." Make a Honcho account if you don't have one, enter your name, click Authorize , and that's it — you're connected. Follow the on-screen steps in the video if anything looks different.
 Copy-paste prompts 

 None this chapter — this one is all clicks, no prompts.

 ✅ Your Day 5 post 

 Post a screenshot of your Hermes Settings → Memory and Context page showing Honcho selected as your memory provider and connected via OAuth.

pages.title: Your AI Project Manager Dashboard
pages.folder: Day 06 — Your AI Project Manager Dashboard
course_path: Day 06 — Your AI Project Manager Dashboard
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Your AI Project Manager Dashboard What you'll have by the end: Multica installed on your computer with your agents auto-detected, plus a deterministic hook that makes Hermes log every single request you give it as a tracked issue on your project board — moved across the board to done, verified live.

 Why this matters: Chat is great for talking to your agent, but it's a terrible way to manage work. A board gives you visibility and control over everything your agents are doing — and with a hook, Hermes updates it for you 100% of the time, no reminders needed.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps One concept first: hooks. A hook is an automatic trigger. Every time Hermes gets a message, starts work, or finishes a segment, the hook fires — automatically, deterministically, 100% of the time. It can't forget and it can't hallucinate. You already have one hook running (the Honcho hook from the memory lesson, which loads your memory every time you message Hermes). Today you're adding a second one: a Multica hook that keeps your project board updated every time you ask Hermes to do something.

 - Go to multica.ai and click Get Started . Multica is free — and it's open source, so you can self-host it later if you want (the GitHub code is linked on the site).
 - Log in with Google. A brand new fresh account is fine.
 - You'll get the choice to continue on the web or download the desktop app. Download it to your computer — that's what you want, because Multica runs alongside your local agents.
 - Open the downloaded Multica app. It auto-detects the runtimes already on your machine — remember, you installed Codex and Hermes at the start of the course, so those show up automatically. Pick Hermes and start exploring. When it asks for permissions so the Multica helper can start doing stuff, allow it (Samin just picked the last option shown — follow the on-screen steps in the video).
 - Look around the app. Under Runtimes you'll see all your agents and which computer they're running on (your MacBook, a Mac Mini, a VPS — teammates' machines show up here too). Under Workspaces you'll see your workspace with an Issues view.
 - Create your first project. There's no project yet, so make one — Samin names his "test project." Later builds in this course will each live as a project on this board. Also note your Inbox : whenever an agent has an update for you, it lands there and you can leave comments.
 - Now make Hermes the project manager. In Multica, go to Settings → API Tokens → Create a new token . Name it Project Manager , then copy the token .
 - Go back to your Hermes chat , paste the API key, and give it the project manager prompt:
 Copy-paste prompt:

 Multica API key: [PASTE YOUR TOKEN HERE]

I want to set it up such that you are my AI project manager and my chief of staff. To do that, I want you to set a hook such that every time I ask you to do something, you update our Multica project board and create an issue for what I'm trying to do. If it pertains to a new project, I want you to make that. But essentially, you are the project manager, and everything I say to you should be tracked inside the Multica board.

Use this API key and set all that in motion, such that we can see a test issue — I want you to create a test thing and I want to see it moved across the board to done. I want to communicate with you through Multica as well. Remember, you are my AI project manager. Use this key to set up all the hooks required so that we can work together in a more organized way. The key word in there is hook — that's what makes this fire every time instead of only when Hermes feels like it.

 - Let it run, then check the board. In Samin's run, Hermes created a Multica project management skill on its own (without being asked), made a project called "Hermes AI Project Manager / Chief of Staff," created test issues, and moved them across the board to Done . Check your Inbox and Projects — you should see the same kind of activity.
 - Verify the hook is real. Ask Hermes a yes/no question:
 Copy-paste prompt:

 Just answer me yes or no: if I ask you to create a website, will you create a Multica issue first that I can track from there? It should say yes — because the hook is set, you never have to say "save this to Multica" again. Every request becomes a tracked issue automatically.

 Copy-paste prompts Project manager setup (with your API token): 

 Multica API key: [PASTE YOUR TOKEN HERE]

I want to set it up such that you are my AI project manager and my chief of staff. To do that, I want you to set a hook such that every time I ask you to do something, you update our Multica project board and create an issue for what I'm trying to do. If it pertains to a new project, I want you to make that. But essentially, you are the project manager, and everything I say to you should be tracked inside the Multica board.

Use this API key and set all that in motion, such that we can see a test issue — I want you to create a test thing and I want to see it moved across the board to done. I want to communicate with you through Multica as well. Remember, you are my AI project manager. Use this key to set up all the hooks required so that we can work together in a more organized way. Hook verification: 

 Just answer me yes or no: if I ask you to create a website, will you create a Multica issue first that I can track from there? ✅ Your Day 6 post Post a screenshot of your Multica board showing the test issue Hermes created and moved to Done — plus Hermes answering "yes" to the verification question if you want bonus points.

  1. Let it run, then check the board. In Samin's run, Hermes created a Multica project management skill on its own (without being asked), made a project called "Hermes AI Project Manager / Chief of Staff," created test issues, and moved them across the board to Done. Check your Inbox and Projects — you should see the same kind of activity.
  1. Verify the hook is real. Ask Hermes a yes/no question:

Copy-paste prompt:

Just answer me yes or no: if I ask you to create a website, will you create a Multica issue first that I can track from there?

It should say yes — because the hook is set, you never have to say "save this to Multica" again. Every request becomes a tracked issue automatically.

Copy-paste prompts

Project manager setup (with your API token):

Multica API key: [PASTE YOUR TOKEN HERE]

I want to set it up such that you are my AI project manager and my chief of staff. To do that, I want you to set a hook such that every time I ask you to do something, you update our Multica project board and create an issue for what I'm trying to do. If it pertains to a new project, I want you to make that. But essentially, you are the project manager, and everything I say to you should be tracked inside the Multica board.

Use this API key and set all that in motion, such that we can see a test issue — I want you to create a test thing and I want to see it moved across the board to done. I want to communicate with you through Multica as well. Remember, you are my AI project manager. Use this key to set up all the hooks required so that we can work together in a more organized way.

Hook verification:

Just answer me yes or no: if I ask you to create a website, will you create a Multica issue first that I can track from there?

✅ Your Day 6 post

Post a screenshot of your Multica board showing the test issue Hermes created and moved to Done — plus Hermes answering "yes" to the verification question if you want bonus points.

rag.search_text:
Your AI Project Manager Dashboard

Day 06 — Your AI Project Manager Dashboard

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Your AI Project Manager Dashboard What you'll have by the end: Multica installed on your computer with your agents auto-detected, plus a deterministic hook that makes Hermes log every single request you give it as a tracked issue on your project board — moved across the board to done, verified live.

 Why this matters: Chat is great for talking to your agent, but it's a terrible way to manage work. A board gives you visibility and control over everything your agents are doing — and with a hook, Hermes updates it for you 100% of the time, no reminders needed.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps One concept first: hooks. A hook is an automatic trigger. Every time Hermes gets a message, starts work, or finishes a segment, the hook fires — automatically, deterministically, 100% of the time. It can't forget and it can't hallucinate. You already have one hook running (the Honcho hook from the memory lesson, which loads your memory every time you message Hermes). Today you're adding a second one: a Multica hook that keeps your project board updated every time you ask Hermes to do something.

 - Go to multica.ai and click Get Started . Multica is free — and it's open source, so you can self-host it later if you want (the GitHub code is linked on the site).
 - Log in with Google. A brand new fresh account is fine.
 - You'll get the choice to continue on the web or download the desktop app. Download it to your computer — that's what you want, because Multica runs alongside your local agents.
 - Open the downloaded Multica app. It auto-detects the runtimes already on your machine — remember, you installed Codex and Hermes at the start of the course, so those show up automatically. Pick Hermes and start exploring. When it asks for permissions so the Multica helper can start doing stuff, allow it (Samin just picked the last option shown — follow the on-screen steps in the video).
 - Look around the app. Under Runtimes you'll see all your agents and which computer they're running on (your MacBook, a Mac Mini, a VPS — teammates' machines show up here too). Under Workspaces you'll see your workspace with an Issues view.
 - Create your first project. There's no project yet, so make one — Samin names his "test project." Later builds in this course will each live as a project on this board. Also note your Inbox : whenever an agent has an update for you, it lands there and you can leave comments.
 - Now make Hermes the project manager. In Multica, go to Settings → API Tokens → Create a new token . Name it Project Manager , then copy the token .
 - Go back to your Hermes chat , paste the API key, and give it the project manager prompt:
 Copy-paste prompt:

 Multica API key: [PASTE YOUR TOKEN HERE]

I want to set it up such that you are my AI project manager and my chief of staff. To do that, I want you to set a hook such that every time I ask you to do something, you update our Multica project board and create an issue for what I'm trying to do. If it pertains to a new project, I want you to make that. But essentially, you are the project manager, and everything I say to you should be tracked inside the Multica board.

Use this API key and set all that in motion, such that we can see a test issue — I want you to create a test thing and I want to see it moved across the board to done. I want to communicate with you through Multica as well. Remember, you are my AI project manager. Use this key to set up all the hooks required so that we can work together in a more organized way. The key word in there is hook — that's what makes this fire every time instead of only when Hermes feels like it.

 - Let it run, then check the board. In Samin's run, Hermes created a Multica project management skill on its own (without being asked), made a project called "Hermes AI Project Manager / Chief of Staff," created test issues, and moved them across the board to Done . Check your Inbox and Projects — you should see the same kind of activity.
 - Verify the hook is real. Ask Hermes a yes/no question:
 Copy-paste prompt:

 Just answer me yes or no: if I ask you to create a website, will you create a Multica issue first that I can track from there? It should say yes — because the hook is set, you never have to say "save this to Multica" again. Every request becomes a tracked issue automatically.

 Copy-paste prompts Project manager setup (with your API token): 

 Multica API key: [PASTE YOUR TOKEN HERE]

I want to set it up such that you are my AI project manager and my chief of staff. To do that, I want you to set a hook such that every time I ask you to do something, you update our Multica project board and create an issue for what I'm trying to do. If it pertains to a new project, I want you to make that. But essentially, you are the project manager, and everything I say to you should be tracked inside the Multica board.

Use this API key and set all that in motion, such that we can see a test issue — I want you to create a test thing and I want to see it moved across the board to done. I want to communicate with you through Multica as well. Remember, you are my AI project manager. Use this key to set up all the hooks required so that we can work together in a more organized way. Hook verification: 

 Just answer me yes or no: if I ask you to create a website, will you create a Multica issue first that I can track from there? ✅ Your Day 6 post Post a screenshot of your Multica board showing the test issue Hermes created and moved to Done — plus Hermes answering "yes" to the verification question if you want bonus points.

pages.title: One Agent Becomes a Whole AI Team
pages.folder: Day 07 — One Agent Becomes a Whole AI Team
course_path: Day 07 — One Agent Becomes a Whole AI Team
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 One Agent Becomes a Whole AI Team What you'll have by the end: A second Hermes profile ("personal") with its own personality and a fast model, onboarded to Multica — plus a squad where a leader agent can assign work to other agents *and* human teammates.

 Why this matters: Mixing your personal life, your business, and your clients inside one agent gets messy fast. Profiles give each "AI employee" its own role, model, memory, skills, and tool access — and squads let one leader delegate across all of them.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps - Understand the idea first. One Hermes desktop setup can become multiple AI employees. Each profile gets its own soul (personality), model, connectors, memory, and skills. You could have one personal agent, one for business ops, even one per client.
 - Create a new profile. In Hermes, look at the bottom left — you'll see your current profile. Click Manage profiles , then create a new profile and name it personal .
 - Choose what to clone. You can clone from your default profile (to carry over everything you've built) or start with none . In the video, Samin picks none so you can see a fresh agent from scratch.
 - Reload Hermes. After the reload you'll see both profiles — default and personal — and the personal one wakes up with its own new chats and skills. It's a brand-new Hermes agent.
 - Give it a personality. Open the new profile's settings and set its personality to whatever you like. (Samin makes his a "cat girl" just for fun.)
 - Pick its model — this part matters. Select the model for this profile. You can point it at a Grok API or an OpenAI API, but in the video Samin chooses Codex , picks 5.5 , and sets it to low so this profile is snappy and fast. Hit Apply .
 - Get a Multica API token for this profile. Go to Multica, open Runtimes , then Settings , and create a new API token — make a separate one just for your personal Hermes agent. Create the key and copy it.
 - Onboard the new profile to Multica. Go back to Hermes (in the personal profile) and paste the key with this instruction:
 Copy-paste prompt
This is your API key for Multica: [paste your key]. Go do research and onboard yourself onto Multica so I can see you in the runtimes/agents. Let it work. When it's done, check Runtimes in Multica — you should now see two agents : your default profile and your personal profile.

 - Create a squad. In Multica, go to Squads and create a new squad (Samin names his "Team Claude Club"). You must select a leader — pick your default/chief-of-staff Hermes.
 - Add members. Add the people and agents the leader can assign work to. Samin adds himself, a human teammate (Ed), and the personal profile. Now the squad has three members plus a leader — and the leader can hand tasks to any of them, human or AI.
 - Set routing instructions (or let Hermes write them). Open an agent's detail in the squad and you can add instructions for when to use each model. Samin's real example, his "SAM squad": Hermes (chief of staff, holds all his context) is the leader, and it routes between Claude and Codex since he has both subscriptions. Claude gets planning, product, design, and high-reasoning work. Codex gets coding — it's better at implementing Claude's plans. He didn't write any of those instructions by hand — he just asked Hermes:
 Copy-paste prompt
Set up my squad so that [describe your setup] and I'm using different models for different things. - Give squad members their own skills. Each member can have its own skills and environment variables. Samin's Codex agent carries a bunch of YouTube skills — so when Hermes decides Codex is the best model for a job, it just calls it, and you don't have to think about it.
 - Organize with workspaces. To see this in action, Samin tells Multica to create tasks for his life and business. Two workspaces appear: Hermes course (business — with business follow-ups, outreach to track, and a YouTube pipeline demo) and personal (life — errands and home maintenance like "order vacuum replacement parts" and "summer home maintenance"). Life and business stay separate, and you can chat with or assign tasks to your AI team like they're people.
 Don't stress if the squad routing feels advanced — Samin says it's not necessary for most people yet, and the course goes deeper on it later. For now, get the profile and squad created.

 Copy-paste prompts Onboard a new profile to Multica: 

 This is your API key for Multica: [paste your key]. Go do research and onboard yourself onto Multica so I can see you in the runtimes/agents. Have Hermes build your squad for you: 

 Set up my squad so that [describe your setup] and I'm using different models for different things. Auto-create your workspaces and tasks: 

 Go ahead and create tasks for my life and business. Troubleshooting New profile not showing up? You have to reload Hermes after creating it — the second profile only appears (and wakes up) after the reload.

 ✅ Your Day 7 post Post a screenshot of your Multica Runtimes page showing both agents (default + your new profile), or your new squad with its leader and members — and tell us what personality you gave your second agent.

Let it work. When it's done, check Runtimes in Multica — you should now see two agents: your default profile and your personal profile.

He didn't write any of those instructions by hand — he just asked Hermes:
Copy-paste prompt
Set up my squad so that [describe your setup] and I'm using different models for different things.
  1. Give squad members their own skills. Each member can have its own skills and environment variables. Samin's Codex agent carries a bunch of YouTube skills — so when Hermes decides Codex is the best model for a job, it just calls it, and you don't have to think about it.
  1. Organize with workspaces. To see this in action, Samin tells Multica to create tasks for his life and business. Two workspaces appear: Hermes course (business — with business follow-ups, outreach to track, and a YouTube pipeline demo) and personal (life — errands and home maintenance like "order vacuum replacement parts" and "summer home maintenance"). Life and business stay separate, and you can chat with or assign tasks to your AI team like they're people.

Don't stress if the squad routing feels advanced — Samin says it's not necessary for most people yet, and the course goes deeper on it later. For now, get the profile and squad created.

Copy-paste prompts

Onboard a new profile to Multica:

This is your API key for Multica: [paste your key]. Go do research and onboard yourself onto Multica so I can see you in the runtimes/agents.

Have Hermes build your squad for you:

Set up my squad so that [describe your setup] and I'm using different models for different things.

Auto-create your workspaces and tasks:

Go ahead and create tasks for my life and business.

Troubleshooting

New profile not showing up? You have to reload Hermes after creating it — the second profile only appears (and wakes up) after the reload.

✅ Your Day 7 post

Post a screenshot of your Multica Runtimes page showing both agents (default + your new profile), or your new squad with its leader and members — and tell us what personality you gave your second agent.

transcript.transcript_text: the concept of profiles is basically how we're going to be taking one Hermes desktop setup and turn it into different AI employees. So instead of mixing our personal work and our business work and our client work, we're going to set up different profiles and each will get its own role, model, memory, skills, and tool access. And for example, I can have one personal agent and one for my business ops. If I have 10 business, I can have 10 more. And one for even each client if you want, right? And each one will have their own soul, own model, own connectors, everything. If you want, you can clone them. It's really easy. But let me show you how to set this up. All right. So, setting up a new profile is really easy. On the bottom left right here, you see you already have one profile. Uh, if I hit manage profiles, I already have one profile. And I can hit a new profile by calling it, let's say, personal. Okay. And you can clone from your default. It'll have all the stuff that you're already doing or none. So I'm going to choose none just to show you how it works. So now I have a personal profile. You see, right? Okay. And then what you want to do is reload. Okay. So after you reload, then you'll see there is my default profile and my personal profile. And it's now waking up personal. And now all there's new chats, all the new different skills, everything, right? So you can set this up as a completely new Hermes agent from scratch. So right here, let's go look at So you can see you can have your personality and let's call make this like a I don't know, let's make this catgirl for fun. And um let's see what model is it using. So see, you want to select the model first, which is very important. rag.search_text:
One Agent Becomes a Whole AI Team

Day 07 — One Agent Becomes a Whole AI Team

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 One Agent Becomes a Whole AI Team What you'll have by the end: A second Hermes profile ("personal") with its own personality and a fast model, onboarded to Multica — plus a squad where a leader agent can assign work to other agents *and* human teammates.

 Why this matters: Mixing your personal life, your business, and your clients inside one agent gets messy fast. Profiles give each "AI employee" its own role, model, memory, skills, and tool access — and squads let one leader delegate across all of them.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps - Understand the idea first. One Hermes desktop setup can become multiple AI employees. Each profile gets its own soul (personality), model, connectors, memory, and skills. You could have one personal agent, one for business ops, even one per client.
 - Create a new profile. In Hermes, look at the bottom left — you'll see your current profile. Click Manage profiles , then create a new profile and name it personal .
 - Choose what to clone. You can clone from your default profile (to carry over everything you've built) or start with none . In the video, Samin picks none so you can see a fresh agent from scratch.
 - Reload Hermes. After the reload you'll see both profiles — default and personal — and the personal one wakes up with its own new chats and skills. It's a brand-new Hermes agent.
 - Give it a personality. Open the new profile's settings and set its personality to whatever you like. (Samin makes his a "cat girl" just for fun.)
 - Pick its model — this part matters. Select the model for this profile. You can point it at a Grok API or an OpenAI API, but in the video Samin chooses Codex , picks 5.5 , and sets it to low so this profile is snappy and fast. Hit Apply .
 - Get a Multica API token for this profile. Go to Multica, open Runtimes , then Settings , and create a new API token — make a separate one just for your personal Hermes agent. Create the key and copy it.
 - Onboard the new profile to Multica. Go back to Hermes (in the personal profile) and paste the key with this instruction:
 Copy-paste prompt
This is your API key for Multica: [paste your key]. Go do research and onboard yourself onto Multica so I can see you in the runtimes/agents. Let it work. When it's done, check Runtimes in Multica — you should now see two agents : your default profile and your personal profile.

 - Create a squad. In Multica, go to Squads and create a new squad (Samin names his "Team Claude Club"). You must select a leader — pick your default/chief-of-staff Hermes.
 - Add members. Add the people and agents the leader can assign work to. Samin adds himself, a human teammate (Ed), and the personal profile. Now the squad has three members plus a leader — and the leader can hand tasks to any of them, human or AI.
 - Set routing instructions (or let Hermes write them). Open an agent's detail in the squad and you can add instructions for when to use each model. Samin's real example, his "SAM squad": Hermes (chief of staff, holds all his context) is the leader, and it routes between Claude and Codex since he has both subscriptions. Claude gets planning, product, design, and high-reasoning work. Codex gets coding — it's better at implementing Claude's plans. He didn't write any of those instructions by hand — he just asked Hermes:
 Copy-paste prompt
Set up my squad so that [describe your setup] and I'm using different models for different things. - Give squad members their own skills. Each member can have its own skills and environment variables. Samin's Codex agent carries a bunch of YouTube skills — so when Hermes decides Codex is the best model for a job, it just calls it, and you don't have to think about it.
 - Organize with workspaces. To see this in action, Samin tells Multica to create tasks for his life and business. Two workspaces appear: Hermes course (business — with business follow-ups, outreach to track, and a YouTube pipeline demo) and personal (life — errands and home maintenance like "order vacuum replacement parts" and "summer home maintenance"). Life and business stay separate, and you can chat with or assign tasks to your AI team like they're people.
 Don't stress if the squad routing feels advanced — Samin says it's not necessary for most people yet, and the course goes deeper on it later. For now, get the profile and squad created.

 Copy-paste prompts Onboard a new profile to Multica: 

 This is your API key for Multica: [paste your key]. Go do research and onboard yourself onto Multica so I can see you in the runtimes/agents. Have Hermes build your squad for you: 

 Set up my squad so that [describe your setup] and I'm using different models for different things. Auto-create your workspaces and tasks: 

 Go ahead and create tasks for my life and business. Troubleshooting New profile not showing up? You have to reload Hermes after creating it — the second profile only appears (and wakes up) after the reload.

 ✅ Your Day 7 post Post a screenshot of your Multica Runtimes page showing both agents (default + your new profile), or your new squad with its leader and members — and tell us what personality you gave your second agent.

pages.title: Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps
pages.folder: Day 08 — Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps
course_path: Day 08 — Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps What you'll have by the end: Hermes reads and manages your Google Calendar from any channel — even your phone — plus access to Gmail, Drive, Docs, and Slides through Composio and the Google Workspace CLI.

 Why this matters: Hermes isn't magic. It can't touch Gmail, your calendar, or Google Slides until you hand it the keys. Connectors are those keys — and once you learn the two patterns in this chapter, you can plug Hermes into almost anything.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps The 60-second concept first. A "connector" is just a way for your agent to talk to an outside app. You'll hear three terms — MCP , CLI , and API . They all mean the same basic thing: different ways to communicate with an external application and use its features. In this chapter you'll set up one MCP (Composio) and one CLI (Google Workspace CLI). That's it for theory — now build.

 Part 1: Composio (the MCP way) 

 - Sign up for Composio and log in. It's free. Composio is a gateway: instead of wiring Hermes to Gmail, Calendar, and every other app one by one, Hermes connects to Composio once, and Composio handles the connection to hundreds of apps.
 - In your Composio dashboard, browse the app list. To connect any app (Samin demos this with Linear), just hit Connect and sign in — it's one-click OAuth, which is normally the hard part.
 - For this lesson, find Google Calendar in the list and connect it to your Google account.
 - Now give Hermes access to Composio. Go back to Hermes, make sure you're on the right profile, and start a new session . Then send this:
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, I'm trying to connect to Composio MCP with OAuth. I already have a lot of my stuff connected. Can you give me the link so I can authenticate and you can set up Composio MCP for me? - Hermes will give you an authentication link. Click it, hit Allow access , and wait for the "authorization successful" message. Then go back to Hermes and let it finish the setup.
 - Verify it worked: in Hermes, go to Settings → MCP . You should see Composio listed there.
 - Test it live. Ask Hermes:
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, what do I have for tomorrow in my calendar? Hermes should pull up everything on your calendar. From now on it can read your schedule, move things around, and make appointments for you — through any channel, even from your phone.

 Part 2: Google Workspace CLI (the CLI way) 

 - The Google Workspace CLI lets Hermes work with Google Drive, Google Docs, and Google Slides — and it's even easier to use. It lives on GitHub (the link is below the video). GitHub, if you're new to it, is where everyone stores their code — you'll find lots of CLIs and MCPs there.
 - Copy the GitHub link for the Google Workspace CLI, go back to Hermes, and ask it to install it for you:
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, can you help me install this? [paste the GitHub link] - Let Hermes take it from there — follow along in the video as it works through the setup.
 That's the pattern you'll use forever: find a connector (MCP or CLI), hand the link to Hermes, and let it do the setup.

 Copy-paste prompts Set up Composio MCP: 

 Hey, I'm trying to connect to Composio MCP with OAuth. I already have a lot of my stuff connected. Can you give me the link so I can authenticate and you can set up Composio MCP for me? Test calendar access: 

 Hey, what do I have for tomorrow in my calendar? Install a CLI from GitHub: 

 Hey, can you help me install this? [paste the GitHub link] ✅ Your Day 8 post Post a screenshot of Hermes answering "what do I have tomorrow in my calendar?" with your real schedule (blur anything private) — plus which apps you connected in Composio.

✅ Your Day 8 post

pages.folder_normalized: Day 08 — Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps
pages.location_label: Hermes Agent Course [BETA] → Day 08 — Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps → Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps

rag.search_text:
Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps

Day 08 — Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Connect Your Calendar, Gmail & Apps What you'll have by the end: Hermes reads and manages your Google Calendar from any channel — even your phone — plus access to Gmail, Drive, Docs, and Slides through Composio and the Google Workspace CLI.

 Why this matters: Hermes isn't magic. It can't touch Gmail, your calendar, or Google Slides until you hand it the keys. Connectors are those keys — and once you learn the two patterns in this chapter, you can plug Hermes into almost anything.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps The 60-second concept first. A "connector" is just a way for your agent to talk to an outside app. You'll hear three terms — MCP , CLI , and API . They all mean the same basic thing: different ways to communicate with an external application and use its features. In this chapter you'll set up one MCP (Composio) and one CLI (Google Workspace CLI). That's it for theory — now build.

 Part 1: Composio (the MCP way) 

 - Sign up for Composio and log in. It's free. Composio is a gateway: instead of wiring Hermes to Gmail, Calendar, and every other app one by one, Hermes connects to Composio once, and Composio handles the connection to hundreds of apps.
 - In your Composio dashboard, browse the app list. To connect any app (Samin demos this with Linear), just hit Connect and sign in — it's one-click OAuth, which is normally the hard part.
 - For this lesson, find Google Calendar in the list and connect it to your Google account.
 - Now give Hermes access to Composio. Go back to Hermes, make sure you're on the right profile, and start a new session . Then send this:
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, I'm trying to connect to Composio MCP with OAuth. I already have a lot of my stuff connected. Can you give me the link so I can authenticate and you can set up Composio MCP for me? - Hermes will give you an authentication link. Click it, hit Allow access , and wait for the "authorization successful" message. Then go back to Hermes and let it finish the setup.
 - Verify it worked: in Hermes, go to Settings → MCP . You should see Composio listed there.
 - Test it live. Ask Hermes:
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, what do I have for tomorrow in my calendar? Hermes should pull up everything on your calendar. From now on it can read your schedule, move things around, and make appointments for you — through any channel, even from your phone.

 Part 2: Google Workspace CLI (the CLI way) 

 - The Google Workspace CLI lets Hermes work with Google Drive, Google Docs, and Google Slides — and it's even easier to use. It lives on GitHub (the link is below the video). GitHub, if you're new to it, is where everyone stores their code — you'll find lots of CLIs and MCPs there.
 - Copy the GitHub link for the Google Workspace CLI, go back to Hermes, and ask it to install it for you:
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, can you help me install this? [paste the GitHub link] - Let Hermes take it from there — follow along in the video as it works through the setup.
 That's the pattern you'll use forever: find a connector (MCP or CLI), hand the link to Hermes, and let it do the setup.

 Copy-paste prompts Set up Composio MCP: 

 Hey, I'm trying to connect to Composio MCP with OAuth. I already have a lot of my stuff connected. Can you give me the link so I can authenticate and you can set up Composio MCP for me? Test calendar access: 

 Hey, what do I have for tomorrow in my calendar? Install a CLI from GitHub: 

 Hey, can you help me install this? [paste the GitHub link] ✅ Your Day 8 post Post a screenshot of Hermes answering "what do I have tomorrow in my calendar?" with your real schedule (blur anything private) — plus which apps you connected in Composio.

pages.title: Your Daily Morning Briefing on Autopilot
pages.folder: Day 09 — Your Daily Morning Briefing on Autopilot
course_path: Day 09 — Your Daily Morning Briefing on Autopilot
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Your Daily Morning Briefing on Autopilot What you'll have by the end: A morning briefing that lands in your Telegram every weekday at 8 a.m. — your calendar, urgent emails with drafted replies, a Slack summary, and trending topics — running on autopilot and packaged as a reusable skill. Plus the 5-step recipe you'll use for every autopilot after this.

 Why this matters: This is the first time Hermes works for you *without you asking*. One message today, and it keeps delivering every morning while you sleep.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps - Understand what a cron job is. A cron job is a schedule that tells Hermes to run a task on repeat, automatically. Samin shows a few examples of what people build with them: a weekly client report every Friday (pulled from Slack, Google Drive, Google Docs, and project boards), daily trend research for your niche, competitor price monitoring that alerts you to changes, and an invoice checker that connects to Stripe or QuickBooks and drafts follow-ups for unpaid invoices. You're about to build your first one.
 - Open Telegram. You'll set this whole thing up from your phone by talking to Hermes — no code, no settings screens. Samin dictates the request as one voice message.
 - Send Hermes the morning briefing request. Speak it or paste it. Here's the prompt Samin uses (lightly cleaned up):
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, every weekday at 8 a.m., create a morning briefing for me.

Use my calendar to show me what meetings I have coming up, and do some research on what I should know before them. Use my Gmail to find any urgent or important emails from the last 24 hours — make a draft reply for each and send me links so I can look at them. Give me a brief overview of what my team is talking about in Slack and whether I need to do anything.

Keep the whole briefing short and practical: today's schedule, urgent emails, any important docs, any follow-up tasks, and anything I should prepare before meetings. Send it to me here. If there's nothing important, keep it short.

Also, use Twitter to find the trending topics in AI that I can talk about for my channel.

Right now: send me an example morning briefing and set up the cron job. That last line matters — asking for an example briefing right now means you can check the output today instead of waiting until tomorrow morning.

 - Review the example briefing. In the video, Hermes sends back a full briefing: calendar items, emails (Samin has his scrubbed and replaced with fake ones for privacy), a Slack overview, and trending topics — including news released that same day, so the research is genuinely fresh.
 - See the job in Hermes. Open the Hermes app and look at the bottom left — there's a cron job section . You'll see your morning briefing listed with when it runs.
 - See it in Multica too. Open Multica, go to your project, and you'll see a daily briefing autopilot with its next run time (in the video: July 1st, 8 a.m.). "Autopilot" and "cron job" are the same thing — Multica is just your one place to manage them all, and it works in your web browser on any computer.
 - Massage it until it's right. You almost never nail it on the first go. Reply to Hermes in Telegram with tweaks: "change this language," "drop that detail," "add this." Keep going until the briefing looks the way you want.
 - Turn it into a skill. Once you're happy, tell Hermes:
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, can you make this morning briefing into a skill. I want you to also put it in Multica so I can see it there. A skill is a packaged playbook. The research changes every day, but the format stays the same — and now you can reuse it or share it with your team.

 - Learn the 5-step recipe. Every autopilot you'll ever build follows the same loop: get an agent (Hermes) → connect the apps it needs (Google, Slack, whatever) → massage it until the output is good → set it into a loop (cron job) → package it as a skill so you can share it. That's an agentic workflow — Hermes researching, curating, creating, reviewing, and delivering, all without you asking.
 - Tour the skill library. In Hermes Desktop, browse your skills — Hermes comes pre-built with a lot of them, plus cron jobs and tool sets ready to go (Samin searches his X without configuring anything). Don't worry about having too many skills: Hermes finds them through an index, so lots of skills stays fast. Then check Multica's Skills tab — your morning briefing skill is saved there too.
 Copy-paste prompts The morning briefing autopilot — see step 3 above.

 Package it as a skill — see step 8 above.

 Troubleshooting The first briefing isn't quite right? That's normal — Samin says it's "not that you're always gonna get it good in the first go." Just talk to Hermes in Telegram: tell it what to change, what to cut, what to add. Massage it before you turn it into a skill.

 Want to check what's actually scheduled? Look at the cron job section in the bottom left of the Hermes app, or open your project in Multica to see the autopilot and its next run time.

 ✅ Your Day 9 post Post a screenshot of your morning briefing in Telegram (blur anything private!) plus your autopilot showing in Hermes or Multica — and tell us one thing you'd add to your briefing.

  1. Turn it into a skill. Once you're happy, tell Hermes:

Hey, can you make this morning briefing into a skill. I want you to also put it in Multica so I can see it there.

A skill is a packaged playbook. The research changes every day, but the format stays the same — and now you can reuse it or share it with your team.

  1. Learn the 5-step recipe. Every autopilot you'll ever build follows the same loop: get an agent (Hermes) → connect the apps it needs (Google, Slack, whatever) → massage it until the output is good → set it into a loop (cron job) → package it as a skill so you can share it. That's an agentic workflow — Hermes researching, curating, creating, reviewing, and delivering, all without you asking.
  1. Tour the skill library. In Hermes Desktop, browse your skills — Hermes comes pre-built with a lot of them, plus cron jobs and tool sets ready to go (Samin searches his X without configuring anything). Don't worry about having too many skills: Hermes finds them through an index, so lots of skills stays fast. Then check Multica's Skills tab — your morning briefing skill is saved there too.

Copy-paste prompts

The morning briefing autopilot — see step 3 above.

Package it as a skill — see step 8 above.

Troubleshooting

The first briefing isn't quite right? That's normal — Samin says it's "not that you're always gonna get it good in the first go." Just talk to Hermes in Telegram: tell it what to change, what to cut, what to add. Massage it before you turn it into a skill.

Want to check what's actually scheduled? Look at the cron job section in the bottom left of the Hermes app, or open your project in Multica to see the autopilot and its next run time.

✅ Your Day 9 post

Post a screenshot of your morning briefing in Telegram (blur anything private!) plus your autopilot showing in Hermes or Multica — and tell us one thing you'd add to your briefing.

You can see in Hermes desktop, you have access to all your different skills. All these are different skills that you can use. And Hermes comes pre-built with a lot of them. All right? And this is how we make our agents better. And having more skills isn't a problem because how it looks

for skills is there's an index. So it's actually very efficient to have a lot of skills. So don't worry about having a lot of skills. You'll be fine. But you can see Hermes is so good out of the box because they already prepackage a lot of useful skills for you. So you can very simply use Hermes out of the box and you get a lot better experience, right? And you can see all the skills, cron jobs, all these tools, the tool sets are all pre-built in like I can search my X without having to configure anything, etc. And now if I go back to my Maltica and if I go to skills, you'll also see that morning briefing skill is over here, right? So everything that we're doing with our Hermes is saved in two places. And the nice part about uh this multi thing is you can use this on your desktop in the web browser and it becomes really easy to use, right? Okay. So that's what I wanted to teach you about skills and we're going to be diving a lot deeper into Agentic Workflows and building out things that are super useful. So let's get into

rag.search_text:
Your Daily Morning Briefing on Autopilot

Day 09 — Your Daily Morning Briefing on Autopilot

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Your Daily Morning Briefing on Autopilot What you'll have by the end: A morning briefing that lands in your Telegram every weekday at 8 a.m. — your calendar, urgent emails with drafted replies, a Slack summary, and trending topics — running on autopilot and packaged as a reusable skill. Plus the 5-step recipe you'll use for every autopilot after this.

 Why this matters: This is the first time Hermes works for you *without you asking*. One message today, and it keeps delivering every morning while you sleep.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps - Understand what a cron job is. A cron job is a schedule that tells Hermes to run a task on repeat, automatically. Samin shows a few examples of what people build with them: a weekly client report every Friday (pulled from Slack, Google Drive, Google Docs, and project boards), daily trend research for your niche, competitor price monitoring that alerts you to changes, and an invoice checker that connects to Stripe or QuickBooks and drafts follow-ups for unpaid invoices. You're about to build your first one.
 - Open Telegram. You'll set this whole thing up from your phone by talking to Hermes — no code, no settings screens. Samin dictates the request as one voice message.
 - Send Hermes the morning briefing request. Speak it or paste it. Here's the prompt Samin uses (lightly cleaned up):
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, every weekday at 8 a.m., create a morning briefing for me.

Use my calendar to show me what meetings I have coming up, and do some research on what I should know before them. Use my Gmail to find any urgent or important emails from the last 24 hours — make a draft reply for each and send me links so I can look at them. Give me a brief overview of what my team is talking about in Slack and whether I need to do anything.

Keep the whole briefing short and practical: today's schedule, urgent emails, any important docs, any follow-up tasks, and anything I should prepare before meetings. Send it to me here. If there's nothing important, keep it short.

Also, use Twitter to find the trending topics in AI that I can talk about for my channel.

Right now: send me an example morning briefing and set up the cron job. That last line matters — asking for an example briefing right now means you can check the output today instead of waiting until tomorrow morning.

 - Review the example briefing. In the video, Hermes sends back a full briefing: calendar items, emails (Samin has his scrubbed and replaced with fake ones for privacy), a Slack overview, and trending topics — including news released that same day, so the research is genuinely fresh.
 - See the job in Hermes. Open the Hermes app and look at the bottom left — there's a cron job section . You'll see your morning briefing listed with when it runs.
 - See it in Multica too. Open Multica, go to your project, and you'll see a daily briefing autopilot with its next run time (in the video: July 1st, 8 a.m.). "Autopilot" and "cron job" are the same thing — Multica is just your one place to manage them all, and it works in your web browser on any computer.
 - Massage it until it's right. You almost never nail it on the first go. Reply to Hermes in Telegram with tweaks: "change this language," "drop that detail," "add this." Keep going until the briefing looks the way you want.
 - Turn it into a skill. Once you're happy, tell Hermes:
 Copy-paste prompt

Hey, can you make this morning briefing into a skill. I want you to also put it in Multica so I can see it there. A skill is a packaged playbook. The research changes every day, but the format stays the same — and now you can reuse it or share it with your team.

 - Learn the 5-step recipe. Every autopilot you'll ever build follows the same loop: get an agent (Hermes) → connect the apps it needs (Google, Slack, whatever) → massage it until the output is good → set it into a loop (cron job) → package it as a skill so you can share it. That's an agentic workflow — Hermes researching, curating, creating, reviewing, and delivering, all without you asking.
 - Tour the skill library. In Hermes Desktop, browse your skills — Hermes comes pre-built with a lot of them, plus cron jobs and tool sets ready to go (Samin searches his X without configuring anything). Don't worry about having too many skills: Hermes finds them through an index, so lots of skills stays fast. Then check Multica's Skills tab — your morning briefing skill is saved there too.
 Copy-paste prompts The morning briefing autopilot — see step 3 above.

 Package it as a skill — see step 8 above.

 Troubleshooting The first briefing isn't quite right? That's normal — Samin says it's "not that you're always gonna get it good in the first go." Just talk to Hermes in Telegram: tell it what to change, what to cut, what to add. Massage it before you turn it into a skill.

 Want to check what's actually scheduled? Look at the cron job section in the bottom left of the Hermes app, or open your project in Multica to see the autopilot and its next run time.

 ✅ Your Day 9 post Post a screenshot of your morning briefing in Telegram (blur anything private!) plus your autopilot showing in Hermes or Multica — and tell us one thing you'd add to your briefing.

pages.title: Build a Full Website With One Command
pages.folder: Day 10 — Build a Full Website With One Command
course_path: Day 10 — Build a Full Website With One Command
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Build a Full Website With One Command What you'll have by the end: A live, deployed animated website selling AI employees — with copy, images, and video all AI-generated — built from a single /goal command and hosted on a real link you can open on your phone.

 Why this matters: A normal prompt gives you one shot and whatever comes out, comes out. The /goal command gives Hermes an *outcome* and lets it loop — build, review, fix, repeat — until the result is actually good, without you babysitting it.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps - Understand the tool for this build. In Hermes, type /goal . You'll see it described as "set a standing goal Hermes works on across turns until achieved." Unlike a regular prompt, Hermes will keep building, checking its own work, and fixing what's missing until the outcome is done. That's what lets us one-shot a premium site instead of an AI-slop landing page.
 - Install power-up #1: Higgsfield AI (images and video). Sign up at Higgsfield AI, then find the section called MCP and CLI and click Hermes . You'll see the npm install command. Don't run it yourself — copy it, go to your Hermes agent, and say:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, can you install this Higgsfield CLI and help me authenticate it with OAuth? - Approve the OAuth window. A browser window opens — hit Allow . You'll see "device authorized." Hermes should also install the Higgsfield *skill* automatically. If it doesn't, go back to the Higgsfield page, copy the skill setup, and paste it into Hermes.
 - Install power-up #2: Firecrawl (scraping). Firecrawl lets Hermes scrape your own site, monitor it, and pull inspiration from competitor websites. Copy the setup from the Firecrawl page (links are in the video description), then tell Hermes:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, can you install Firecrawl? - Install power-up #3: here.now (free instant hosting). Your finished website — plus its videos and photos — needs to live somewhere. That's here.now: free, instant web hosting for any app or site. Copy the setup from the here.now page (also in the description), paste it into Hermes, and say install this . (Samin uses here.now constantly — for example, his trading bot builds him a live dashboard on here.now so he can check it from Telegram on his phone.)
 - Check your loadout. You should now have all three installed and authenticated: Higgsfield , Firecrawl , and here.now . Hermes needs all three to pull this off.
 - Set the goal. Type /goal in Hermes and give it the full outcome — the offer, the look, the assets, and where to deliver the link (full prompt below). Hermes will reply with something like "goal set, 20 turn budget" and start working.
 - Let it run. Hermes will write the copy, generate photos and video with Higgsfield, check how they look, insert them, scrape for inspiration with Firecrawl, deploy to here.now, and keep looping until it's done. You don't touch anything.
 - Tour the result. When Hermes says it's done, open the here.now link it gives you. In the video, the one-shot result has an animated background, a moving hero graphic ("Hermes hired — your brand's content on autopilot"), AI-written copy, generated section images (clipping videos, writing ads), and Book a Call buttons everywhere.
 - Inspect the generated assets. Go to your Higgsfield account and open your Images tab — you'll see every image Hermes just generated for the site. Check the Videos tab for the video assets (it may have generated some audio too).
 Copy-paste prompts Install Higgsfield: 

 Hey, can you install this Higgsfield CLI and help me authenticate it with OAuth? Install Firecrawl and here.now (paste each tool's setup snippet along with this):

 Hey, can you install Firecrawl? And the other thing I want you to install is here.now — here's the setup, install this. The website goal (run with /goal ):

 I want you to build me a website. Make it an animated website — it should have an animated background and some really good motion in the front. The offer is simple: we're building Hermes agents as 24/7 AI employees for people, focused on building content and lead gen systems. It can create things like Instagram carousels, reels, YouTube videos, clips, and ads, and even scrape what competitors are doing using Firecrawl. The site needs to feel premium and animated — make it black and white like Notion. Go find the Hermes logo and use it everywhere. The call to action is just a book-a-call. Build me that website, please. After you build it, give me the here.now deployed link here so I can check it. Troubleshooting - The Higgsfield skill didn't auto-install? Go back to the Higgsfield MCP and CLI page, copy the skill setup, and paste it straight into Hermes — it will take care of it.
- Authentication stuck? Watch for the browser window Hermes opens — you have to click Allow for the device to be authorized before Higgsfield will work.
- Site looks flat or has no images? Make sure all three power-ups are actually installed — Hermes needs Higgsfield for visuals and here.now for the deploy link. Follow the on-screen steps in the video to confirm each one.
 ✅ Your Day 10 post Post your live here.now link (plus a screenshot of your animated site) in the community — the site Hermes built and deployed for you from one /goal command.

(Samin uses here.now constantly — for example, his trading bot builds him a live dashboard on here.now so he can check it from Telegram on his phone.)
  1. Check your loadout. You should now have all three installed and authenticated: Higgsfield, Firecrawl, and here.now. Hermes needs all three to pull this off.
  1. Set the goal. Type /goal in Hermes and give it the full outcome — the offer, the look, the assets, and where to deliver the link (full prompt below). Hermes will reply with something like "goal set, 20 turn budget" and start working.
  1. Let it run. Hermes will write the copy, generate photos and video with Higgsfield, check how they look, insert them, scrape for inspiration with Firecrawl, deploy to here.now, and keep looping until it's done. You don't touch anything.

In the video, the one-shot result has an animated background, a moving hero graphic ("Hermes hired — your brand's content on autopilot"), AI-written copy, generated section images (clipping videos, writing ads), and Book a Call buttons everywhere.
  1. Inspect the generated assets. Go to your Higgsfield account and open your Images tab — you'll see every image Hermes just generated for the site. Check the Videos tab for the video assets (it may have generated some audio too).

Copy-paste prompts

Install Higgsfield:

Hey, can you install this Higgsfield CLI and help me authenticate it with OAuth?

Install Firecrawl and here.now (paste each tool's setup snippet along with this):

Hey, can you install Firecrawl? And the other thing I want you to install is here.now — here's the setup, install this.

The website goal (run with /goal):

I want you to build me a website. Make it an animated website — it should have an animated background and some really good motion in the front. The offer is simple: we're building Hermes agents as 24/7 AI employees for people, focused on building content and lead gen systems. It can create things like Instagram carousels, reels, YouTube videos, clips, and ads, and even scrape what competitors are doing using Firecrawl. The site needs to feel premium and animated — make it black and white like Notion. Go find the Hermes logo and use it everywhere. The call to action is just a book-a-call. Build me that website, please. After you build it, give me the here.now deployed link here so I can check it.

Troubleshooting

  • The Higgsfield skill didn't auto-install? Go back to the Higgsfield MCP and CLI page, copy the skill setup, and paste it straight into Hermes — it will take care of it.
  • Authentication stuck? Watch for the browser window Hermes opens — you have to click Allow for the device to be authorized before Higgsfield will work.
  • Site looks flat or has no images? Make sure all three power-ups are actually installed — Hermes needs Higgsfield for visuals and here.now for the deploy link. Follow the on-screen steps in the video to confirm each one.

✅ Your Day 10 post

Post your live here.now link (plus a screenshot of your animated site) in the community — the site Hermes built and deployed for you from one /goal command.

transcript.coordinate_system: Original YouTube master video time, not clip-local time
transcript.transcript_text: to be creating an animated website. And this is where things get a bit more advanced because we're going to be doing this with one command, right? rag.search_text:
Build a Full Website With One Command

Day 10 — Build a Full Website With One Command

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Build a Full Website With One Command What you'll have by the end: A live, deployed animated website selling AI employees — with copy, images, and video all AI-generated — built from a single /goal command and hosted on a real link you can open on your phone.

 Why this matters: A normal prompt gives you one shot and whatever comes out, comes out. The /goal command gives Hermes an *outcome* and lets it loop — build, review, fix, repeat — until the result is actually good, without you babysitting it.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps - Understand the tool for this build. In Hermes, type /goal . You'll see it described as "set a standing goal Hermes works on across turns until achieved." Unlike a regular prompt, Hermes will keep building, checking its own work, and fixing what's missing until the outcome is done. That's what lets us one-shot a premium site instead of an AI-slop landing page.
 - Install power-up #1: Higgsfield AI (images and video). Sign up at Higgsfield AI, then find the section called MCP and CLI and click Hermes . You'll see the npm install command. Don't run it yourself — copy it, go to your Hermes agent, and say:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, can you install this Higgsfield CLI and help me authenticate it with OAuth? - Approve the OAuth window. A browser window opens — hit Allow . You'll see "device authorized." Hermes should also install the Higgsfield *skill* automatically. If it doesn't, go back to the Higgsfield page, copy the skill setup, and paste it into Hermes.
 - Install power-up #2: Firecrawl (scraping). Firecrawl lets Hermes scrape your own site, monitor it, and pull inspiration from competitor websites. Copy the setup from the Firecrawl page (links are in the video description), then tell Hermes:
 Copy-paste prompt
Hey, can you install Firecrawl? - Install power-up #3: here.now (free instant hosting). Your finished website — plus its videos and photos — needs to live somewhere. That's here.now: free, instant web hosting for any app or site. Copy the setup from the here.now page (also in the description), paste it into Hermes, and say install this . (Samin uses here.now constantly — for example, his trading bot builds him a live dashboard on here.now so he can check it from Telegram on his phone.)
 - Check your loadout. You should now have all three installed and authenticated: Higgsfield , Firecrawl , and here.now . Hermes needs all three to pull this off.
 - Set the goal. Type /goal in Hermes and give it the full outcome — the offer, the look, the assets, and where to deliver the link (full prompt below). Hermes will reply with something like "goal set, 20 turn budget" and start working.
 - Let it run. Hermes will write the copy, generate photos and video with Higgsfield, check how they look, insert them, scrape for inspiration with Firecrawl, deploy to here.now, and keep looping until it's done. You don't touch anything.
 - Tour the result. When Hermes says it's done, open the here.now link it gives you. In the video, the one-shot result has an animated background, a moving hero graphic ("Hermes hired — your brand's content on autopilot"), AI-written copy, generated section images (clipping videos, writing ads), and Book a Call buttons everywhere.
 - Inspect the generated assets. Go to your Higgsfield account and open your Images tab — you'll see every image Hermes just generated for the site. Check the Videos tab for the video assets (it may have generated some audio too).
 Copy-paste prompts Install Higgsfield: 

 Hey, can you install this Higgsfield CLI and help me authenticate it with OAuth? Install Firecrawl and here.now (paste each tool's setup snippet along with this):

 Hey, can you install Firecrawl? And the other thing I want you to install is here.now — here's the setup, install this. The website goal (run with /goal ):

 I want you to build me a website. Make it an animated website — it should have an animated background and some really good motion in the front. The offer is simple: we're building Hermes agents as 24/7 AI employees for people, focused on building content and lead gen systems. It can create things like Instagram carousels, reels, YouTube videos, clips, and ads, and even scrape what competitors are doing using Firecrawl. The site needs to feel premium and animated — make it black and white like Notion. Go find the Hermes logo and use it everywhere. The call to action is just a book-a-call. Build me that website, please. After you build it, give me the here.now deployed link here so I can check it. Troubleshooting - The Higgsfield skill didn't auto-install? Go back to the Higgsfield MCP and CLI page, copy the skill setup, and paste it straight into Hermes — it will take care of it.
- Authentication stuck? Watch for the browser window Hermes opens — you have to click Allow for the device to be authorized before Higgsfield will work.
- Site looks flat or has no images? Make sure all three power-ups are actually installed — Hermes needs Higgsfield for visuals and here.now for the deploy link. Follow the on-screen steps in the video to confirm each one.
 ✅ Your Day 10 post Post your live here.now link (plus a screenshot of your animated site) in the community — the site Hermes built and deployed for you from one /goal command.

pages.title: Make Money: Property Marketing Kits
pages.folder: Day 11 — Make Money: Property Marketing Kits
course_path: Day 11 — Make Money: Property Marketing Kits
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Make Money: Property Marketing Kits What you'll have by the end 

 A finished property marketing kit — staged photos, an AI tour video, and a listing website — for a real property, plus a Firecrawl-scraped list of realtors with their contact info. That's a package you can sell at $250 to $1,000 per listing .

 Why this matters 

 This is Build 3: the first play where Hermes stops costing you money and starts making it. Real estate is full of listings with photos that make it impossible to picture living there — and your agent can now fix that at scale.

 Watch the video, then follow these steps 

 - Understand the offer first. Most listings have bland photos, empty rooms, and flat 2D floor plans. Your Hermes agent can now generate videos, generate photos, scrape the web with Firecrawl, and review its own work — so it can turn one ugly listing into a full marketing kit: a 3D-style designed floor plan you can actually "walk through," staged photos of empty rooms, landscaped versions of barren land, tour videos, and a listing website. Realtors love this, and you can charge $250–$1,000 per listing depending on how big it is.
 - Grab the skill. In the community's skills vault, find the skill called property-marketing-kit . This is a Claude Code skill that handles the whole kit-building process for you. Copy it.
 - Tell Hermes to install it in both places. Paste the skill in and tell your agent to install it in both Multica and Hermes , and to use it to build a marketing kit for a property.
 - Pick a real property and send the full request as a goal. Samin uses an example New York listing. Give your agent the listing link plus the lead-scraping job, and mark it as a goal (remember /goal from Build 2 — this task takes a while, so it needs to run long):
 Copy-paste prompt

Go find properties in New York, and for those apartments I want you to generate property marketing kits. Make sure you install the property-marketing-kit skill in both Multica and Hermes, and use it to build the kit.

Here's an example property: [paste listing link]. Build out a property marketing kit for this, please.

I also want you to generate a list of other properties — use Firecrawl to scrape them so I can send the kit to the realtors. Make sure you find the email address for the company hosting each listing, or a phone number, and also the property link. Get that back to me, please.

Make this a goal, because this is going to take a while. - Let it run. This is a long job — walk away and come back when the agent reports in.
 - Review the finished kit. When Hermes replies, it opens the kit folder in Finder. Samin's kit included staged photos of the property, "host" photos of him showing the place around (the skill automatically used his Higgsfield character), and tour videos — a bedroom clip and a kitchen clip with him presenting the space.
