pages.html:
🎬 Watch the video above, then follow the steps below.

How I Built Jarvis (And How You Can)

What you'll have by the end: The complete Jarvis recipe — all five components mapped to things you've already built in this course — plus the shortcut: a pre-packaged Jarvis waiting for you in the Skool community.

Why this matters: Jarvis looks like magic, but it's really just five pieces you already know snapped together. Once you see the recipe, you can build it — and you could even sell it to someone else.

Watch the video, then follow these steps

Jarvis is the same Hermes you've been building all course — your AI chief of staff on your Mac. The upgrades are a good-looking interface, a cloned voice that talks back to you, calendar access, and context on your life. Here's how the five ingredients stack up.

  1. Ingredient 1: Life context with Honcho. This is the memory you set up early in the course. Because Hermes knows what's important to you and what you need to be doing — like the top three things Samin tells it before bed — it can surface those in your morning briefing. If your Honcho memory is running, this piece is already done.
  1. Ingredient 2: Calendar via Composio + your GCal MCP. With Composio and the Google Calendar MCP connected, Jarvis can automatically organize your calendar. Again — you built this connection earlier. Check it off.
  1. Ingredient 3: Computer use. You set this up early on too. Computer use is how Hermes opens tabs and applications on your computer — it has access to Chrome and your apps, so it can open things up and show you what it needs to show you.
  1. Ingredient 4: The voice. Samin's voice was done through ElevenLabs (he even cloned Paul Bettany's voice for fun). If you don't want to use ElevenLabs and want to keep it local, use Voicebox instead. Either way, both are driven through Hermes Voice, which is already built in — you just give it your key and it handles the rest. Follow the on-screen steps in the video for where the key goes.
  1. Ingredient 5: The HUD (the interface). This comes last because the four pieces above are the core of everything. To build the look, take screenshots of a vibe you like, add what's important to you, and hand it to Claude Design to set the look. Give it your screenshot with a prompt like this:

Copy-paste prompt

Here's a screenshot of a vibe I like. Design an interface for me.
  1. Build it with Fable through your squad. Once Claude Design has the look, get Fable to build it. The trick: use your Hermes as the orchestrator. Go back to your squads (remember, Hermes is your leader). Whenever you have a design project or something really intensive, hand it off to Claude — tell Hermes something like "hey, use Claude to do this." From there it uses Fable on high mode to build and plan everything, and it can use Codex for some of the coding work.
  1. Put it together. Now your Jarvis is connected to Hermes — it has a voice and its own computer, and it can speak back and forth with you. That's the whole machine.

Troubleshooting

Be honest with yourself here: this build is not trivial. Samin says it straight — it took a lot of time, a lot of tokens, and plenty of debugging. If you want to skip the pain, he packaged the whole thing and put it inside the Skool community. You don't have to use it, but if you just want it pre-packaged, it's down there for you — and there's a team inside the Skool that helps you set it up if you're brand new. Grab it, save your time, and spend your energy customizing it instead of debugging it.

✅ Your Day 17 post

Post your Jarvis plan: which voice you're going with (ElevenLabs or Voicebox), which of the five ingredients you already have running, and whether you're building from scratch or grabbing the pre-packaged version.

pages.title: Sell What You Built: Pricing & Offers
pages.folder: Day 18 — Sell What You Built: Pricing & Offers
course_path: Day 18 — Sell What You Built: Pricing & Offers
pages.text:
🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Sell What You Built: Pricing & Offers What you'll have by the end: A complete price list and offer script for every system you built in this course — ready to pitch to a real business this week.

 Why this matters: Mark Cuban says the winners of this wave are the integrators — people who learn AI, then walk into companies and implement it for them. There are 33 million companies in the US, 30 million are solopreneurs, and millions more have 1 to 500 employees with no AI budget and no AI experts. Somebody has to do it for them. That's you.

 The three questions Before selling anything, answer three things. What can I sell? Everything you built in this course is a product — pick what aligns with you and the customers around you. What do I charge? And how do I approach people? The menu below answers all three.

 The offer menu 1. The AI Employee Install — $1,500 + retainer. Set up a Hermes bot for a non-technical business owner: install it on their VPS, Mac Mini, or a spare computer, connect their channels, give it memory, project tracking, and Google. The frame: *"A 24/7 AI employee that already knows your business because it learns over time. Live in 7 days. You never touch the tech — or the setup is free."* Then add a retainer to improve it over time.

 2. The Executive Assistant — $500. Evolve the install into an assistant that runs on autopilot: add Composio, connect the MCPs, set up the cron jobs, and they get morning briefings on their phone. Perfect for busy founders, executives, and agency owners — anyone who starts their day with a million emails. It's the cheapest yes in your lineup , so it's the perfect first sale. Demo your own setup on the call, then upgrade them to the full AI employee later. The frame: they start every morning with a mess — now their day is planned, emails are pre-drafted, and they're caught up on their team. *"In your pocket at 8 a.m. — or you don't pay."*

 3. Animated Websites — $250–500 a pop. More of a lead magnet. Build it with the /goal command and deploy fast to show your capabilities. Find anyone with a bad website: *"This is a premium animated website that sells your offer — the copy is much better too. Live in 24 to 48 hours."* Bonus: they can message the Telegram bot and it changes the site instantly. This offer has been working really well.

 4. Property Marketing Kits — $250 per listing. Use Firecrawl to scrape realtor listings, generate the staged photos and videos with Higgsfield (it's unlimited), and email the finished kit to the agent. Build the kit first and send it for free — don't ask for anything. If they want one for every listing, or their whole brokerage does, put it on autopilot: find new realtors, make kits, keep sending. What you're really selling: *"Twice the interest on your listing in 7 days"* — and agents have lots of listings, so $250 each stacks fast.

 5. Carousels on Autopilot. The start of the content engine — and one of the easiest to deliver. It researches trends, writes in the client's voice, designs the carousel, schedules through Blotato, posts everywhere, and drips daily. The client approves from their phone. If they had zero social presence, now they have one. Every week you show them: *"Look how much content I made you — it just makes sense to keep going."* Content is the lifeblood of a business.

 6. The Content Repurposing Octopus — $1,000–3,000/month. The high-ticket one, for bigger YouTubers or anyone with a business channel. The offer: *"You film once a week — it shows up everywhere, daily, automatically."* One piece of content becomes 30. If they already have a YouTube channel, just do it for them and let them see all the new content in the first 72 hours before they even pay .

 7. Reel Editing. Someone films raw talking heads? Cut the ums and ahs, add B-roll, send it back for review. Creators and founders love this — nobody wants to go back and forth with editors. It costs you pennies and minutes per reel, and it's the perfect bolt-on to offers 5 and 6: fold it into the octopus or charge per reel. The script: *"Film the scripts my agent gives you, get edited, captioned reels back in 24 hours. No editors. Your first one's free."*

 8. The Ecom Ad Factory. Run competitor ad research with the skills from the course, build the images and videos, and use the Facebook MCP to manage ads. The secret: the only thing brands really want is fresh new creatives . Make those, hand them over, and you've got a great offer.

 9. The Community Manager — $500, or per resolved incident. Everyone needs customer support. A bot in their email, Skool, or Discord where Hermes checks in on a cron is super simple to build, and course creators and community owners feel this pain daily. The frame: *"Every member answered within 24 hours, in your voice, without you ever touching your notifications."*

 10. The Premium Builds. For people with more money: the trading bot (help them build out their strategy), the longevity coach (an accountability coach making sure they take their vitamins and hit the gym — people love that on its own), and Jarvis + Agent Club — the cool, performative one. It's the demo that closes the call.

 Pricing principles - Price the outcome, not your hours. They're buying results, not your time.
- Setup fee + retainer is the way to go. The setup fee covers your time, and retainers stack month after month.
- Always have a risk reverser. Especially on your first deal — the risk shouldn't be on the client to keep working with you. "Live in 7 days or it's free."
- Build it for yourself first. Selling a content engine or octopus? Use it yourself, make sure it works, then reach out.
- Send people free stuff. A free property kit, free 72 hours of content, a free first reel. Let the work sell itself.
- Anchor to the human alternative. Compare your price to what they'd pay actual people — and how much more scale and volume they get from you.

pages.html:
🎬 Watch the video above, then follow the steps below.

Sell What You Built: Pricing & Offers

What you'll have by the end: A complete price list and offer script for every system you built in this course — ready to pitch to a real business this week.

Why this matters: Mark Cuban says the winners of this wave are the integrators — people who learn AI, then walk into companies and implement it for them. There are 33 million companies in the US, 30 million are solopreneurs, and millions more have 1 to 500 employees with no AI budget and no AI experts. Somebody has to do it for them. That's you.

The three questions

Before selling anything, answer three things. What can I sell? Everything you built in this course is a product — pick what aligns with you and the customers around you. What do I charge? And how do I approach people? The menu below answers all three.

The offer menu

1. The AI Employee Install — $1,500 + retainer. Set up a Hermes bot for a non-technical business owner: install it on their VPS, Mac Mini, or a spare computer, connect their channels, give it memory, project tracking, and Google. The frame: *"A 24/7 AI employee that already knows your business because it learns over time. Live in 7 days. You never touch the tech — or the setup is free."* Then add a retainer to improve it over time.

2. The Executive Assistant — $500. Evolve the install into an assistant that runs on autopilot: add Composio, connect the MCPs, set up the cron jobs, and they get morning briefings on their phone. Perfect for busy founders, executives, and agency owners — anyone who starts their day with a million emails. It's the cheapest yes in your lineup, so it's the perfect first sale. Demo your own setup on the call, then upgrade them to the full AI employee later. The frame: they start every morning with a mess — now their day is planned, emails are pre-drafted, and they're caught up on their team. *"In your pocket at 8 a.m. — or you don't pay."*

3. Animated Websites — $250–500 a pop. More of a lead magnet. Build it with the /goal command and deploy fast to show your capabilities. Find anyone with a bad website: *"This is a premium animated website that sells your offer — the copy is much better too. Live in 24 to 48 hours."* Bonus: they can message the Telegram bot and it changes the site instantly. This offer has been working really well.

4. Property Marketing Kits — $250 per listing. Use Firecrawl to scrape realtor listings, generate the staged photos and videos with Higgsfield (it's unlimited), and email the finished kit to the agent. Build the kit first and send it for free — don't ask for anything. If they want one for every listing, or their whole brokerage does, put it on autopilot: find new realtors, make kits, keep sending. What you're really selling: *"Twice the interest on your listing in 7 days"* — and agents have lots of listings, so $250 each stacks fast.

5. Carousels on Autopilot. The start of the content engine — and one of the easiest to deliver. It researches trends, writes in the client's voice, designs the carousel, schedules through Blotato, posts everywhere, and drips daily. The client approves from their phone. If they had zero social presence, now they have one. Every week you show them: *"Look how much content I made you — it just makes sense to keep going."* Content is the lifeblood of a business.

6. The Content Repurposing Octopus — $1,000–3,000/month. The high-ticket one, for bigger YouTubers or anyone with a business channel. The offer: *"You film once a week — it shows up everywhere, daily, automatically."* One piece of content becomes 30. If they already have a YouTube channel, just do it for them and let them see all the new content in the first 72 hours before they even pay.

7. Reel Editing. Someone films raw talking heads? Cut the ums and ahs, add B-roll, send it back for review. Creators and founders love this — nobody wants to go back and forth with editors. It costs you pennies and minutes per reel, and it's the perfect bolt-on to offers 5 and 6: fold it into the octopus or charge per reel. The script: *"Film the scripts my agent gives you, get edited, captioned reels back in 24 hours. No editors. Your first one's free."*

8. The Ecom Ad Factory. Run competitor ad research with the skills from the course, build the images and videos, and use the Facebook MCP to manage ads. The secret: the only thing brands really want is fresh new creatives. Make those, hand them over, and you've got a great offer.

9. The Community Manager — $500, or per resolved incident. Everyone needs customer support. A bot in their email, Skool, or Discord where Hermes checks in on a cron is super simple to build, and course creators and community owners feel this pain daily. The frame: *"Every member answered within 24 hours, in your voice, without you ever touching your notifications."*

10. The Premium Builds. For people with more money: the trading bot (help them build out their strategy), the longevity coach (an accountability coach making sure they take their vitamins and hit the gym — people love that on its own), and Jarvis + Agent Club — the cool, performative one. It's the demo that closes the call.

Pricing principles

  • Price the outcome, not your hours. They're buying results, not your time.
  • Setup fee + retainer is the way to go. The setup fee covers your time, and retainers stack month after month.
  • Always have a risk reverser. Especially on your first deal — the risk shouldn't be on the client to keep working with you. "Live in 7 days or it's free."
  • Build it for yourself first. Selling a content engine or octopus? Use it yourself, make sure it works, then reach out.
  • Send people free stuff. A free property kit, free 72 hours of content, a free first reel. Let the work sell itself.
  • Anchor to the human alternative. Compare your price to what they'd pay actual people — and how much more scale and volume they get from you.

If you get stuck on any of these, Samin is inside Claude Club to help you out.

✅ Your Day 18 post

Post which offer you're picking and who your first prospect is.

rag.search_text:
Sell What You Built: Pricing & Offers

Day 18 — Sell What You Built: Pricing & Offers

🎬 Watch the video above , then follow the steps below.

 Sell What You Built: Pricing & Offers What you'll have by the end: A complete price list and offer script for every system you built in this course — ready to pitch to a real business this week.

 Why this matters: Mark Cuban says the winners of this wave are the integrators — people who learn AI, then walk into companies and implement it for them. There are 33 million companies in the US, 30 million are solopreneurs, and millions more have 1 to 500 employees with no AI budget and no AI experts. Somebody has to do it for them. That's you.

 The three questions Before selling anything, answer three things. What can I sell? Everything you built in this course is a product — pick what aligns with you and the customers around you. What do I charge? And how do I approach people? The menu below answers all three.

 The offer menu 1. The AI Employee Install — $1,500 + retainer. Set up a Hermes bot for a non-technical business owner: install it on their VPS, Mac Mini, or a spare computer, connect their channels, give it memory, project tracking, and Google. The frame: *"A 24/7 AI employee that already knows your business because it learns over time. Live in 7 days. You never touch the tech — or the setup is free."* Then add a retainer to improve it over time.

 2. The Executive Assistant — $500. Evolve the install into an assistant that runs on autopilot: add Composio, connect the MCPs, set up the cron jobs, and they get morning briefings on their phone. Perfect for busy founders, executives, and agency owners — anyone who starts their day with a million emails. It's the cheapest yes in your lineup , so it's the perfect first sale. Demo your own setup on the call, then upgrade them to the full AI employee later. The frame: they start every morning with a mess — now their day is planned, emails are pre-drafted, and they're caught up on their team. *"In your pocket at 8 a.m. — or you don't pay."*

 3. Animated Websites — $250–500 a pop. More of a lead magnet. Build it with the /goal command and deploy fast to show your capabilities. Find anyone with a bad website: *"This is a premium animated website that sells your offer — the copy is much better too. Live in 24 to 48 hours."* Bonus: they can message the Telegram bot and it changes the site instantly. This offer has been working really well.

 4. Property Marketing Kits — $250 per listing. Use Firecrawl to scrape realtor listings, generate the staged photos and videos with Higgsfield (it's unlimited), and email the finished kit to the agent. Build the kit first and send it for free — don't ask for anything. If they want one for every listing, or their whole brokerage does, put it on autopilot: find new realtors, make kits, keep sending. What you're really selling: *"Twice the interest on your listing in 7 days"* — and agents have lots of listings, so $250 each stacks fast.

 5. Carousels on Autopilot. The start of the content engine — and one of the easiest to deliver. It researches trends, writes in the client's voice, designs the carousel, schedules through Blotato, posts everywhere, and drips daily. The client approves from their phone. If they had zero social presence, now they have one. Every week you show them: *"Look how much content I made you — it just makes sense to keep going."* Content is the lifeblood of a business.

 6. The Content Repurposing Octopus — $1,000–3,000/month. The high-ticket one, for bigger YouTubers or anyone with a business channel. The offer: *"You film once a week — it shows up everywhere, daily, automatically."* One piece of content becomes 30. If they already have a YouTube channel, just do it for them and let them see all the new content in the first 72 hours before they even pay .

 7. Reel Editing. Someone films raw talking heads? Cut the ums and ahs, add B-roll, send it back for review. Creators and founders love this — nobody wants to go back and forth with editors. It costs you pennies and minutes per reel, and it's the perfect bolt-on to offers 5 and 6: fold it into the octopus or charge per reel. The script: *"Film the scripts my agent gives you, get edited, captioned reels back in 24 hours. No editors. Your first one's free."*

 8. The Ecom Ad Factory. Run competitor ad research with the skills from the course, build the images and videos, and use the Facebook MCP to manage ads. The secret: the only thing brands really want is fresh new creatives . Make those, hand them over, and you've got a great offer.

 9. The Community Manager — $500, or per resolved incident. Everyone needs customer support. A bot in their email, Skool, or Discord where Hermes checks in on a cron is super simple to build, and course creators and community owners feel this pain daily. The frame: *"Every member answered within 24 hours, in your voice, without you ever touching your notifications."*

 10. The Premium Builds. For people with more money: the trading bot (help them build out their strategy), the longevity coach (an accountability coach making sure they take their vitamins and hit the gym — people love that on its own), and Jarvis + Agent Club — the cool, performative one. It's the demo that closes the call.

 Pricing principles - Price the outcome, not your hours. They're buying results, not your time.
- Setup fee + retainer is the way to go. The setup fee covers your time, and retainers stack month after month.
- Always have a risk reverser. Especially on your first deal — the risk shouldn't be on the client to keep working with you. "Live in 7 days or it's free."
- Build it for yourself first. Selling a content engine or octopus? Use it yourself, make sure it works, then reach out.
- Send people free stuff. A free property kit, free 72 hours of content, a free first reel. Let the work sell itself.
- Anchor to the human alternative. Compare your price to what they'd pay actual people — and how much more scale and volume they get from you.
 If you get stuck on any of these, Samin is inside Claude Club to help you out.

 ✅ Your Day 18 post Post which offer you're picking and who your first prospect is.

ingest_notes: Upsert by page_id; re-embed only when content_hash changes.
Chunk text and transcript_text separately with page_type metadata.
list-lessons may miss root pages — pass --extra-id as needed.
metadata.resources often empty — parse html/text links.

course_specific_notes: Treat day lesson body and sliced lesson transcript as separate chunk families.
Resources pages should retain links and location metadata.
The long omnibus master video is intentionally not a separate source in this export.

transcript_ingest.method: canonical_page_id_join + exact master-video ranges + deterministic VTT split
transcript_ingest.timestamp_coordinate_system: Original YouTube master video time, not clip-local time
notes: The master transcript is used as a source corpus, but split into the 22 canonical sliced lesson ranges.
Days 01, 05, 14, and 16 contain two lesson sections and are joined by page_id, not day number alone.
Intentional gaps in the canonical course map are not assigned to neighboring lessons.
Timed chunks preserve absolute master timestamps and lesson-local offsets.
